proposals.title: Deconvolving Local Dielectric Screening in Carbon Nanotube Bundles via Multi-Apex Kelvin Probe Force Microscopy
proposals.motivation: The dielectric environment in carbon nanotube (CNT) bundles critically governs charge transport, exciton dynamics, and optical properties in applications from nanoelectronics to nanophotonics. However, experimental techniques lack the spatial resolution and signal specificity to disentangle the local dielectric response of individual nanotubes and their junctions from the collective, mesoscale behavior of the bundle. This gap hinders the rational design of CNT-based devices where electrostatic screening is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: The complex capacitive signal from a multi-apex scanning probe can be computationally deconvolved to isolate the distinct dielectric contributions from individual CNTs and inter-tube junctions within a bundle, revealing how bundle geometry (twist, spacing) dictates local electrostatic screening.
proposals.method: We will employ multi-frequency Kelvin Probe Force Microscopy (KPFM) using custom-fabricated tips with controlled, multi-faceted apex geometries to scan CNT bundles. The measured frequency-dependent capacitance and contact potential difference (CPD) maps will be processed using a blind deconvolution algorithm, informed by a finite-element model of the tip-bundle electrostatic interaction. This model will treat the bundle as a network of 1D conductors with local dielectric tensors, allowing the algorithm to iteratively separate the signal into contributions from individual nanotube surfaces and junction regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, nanoscale maps of dielectric screening within CNT bundles, quantifying the screening efficiency at tube junctions versus sidewalls. It will establish a new SPM-based metrology for correlating bundle morphology with electrostatic properties, directly informing models for charge injection and exciton diffusion in CNT-based optoelectronic devices.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond the limitation of requiring atomically defined single-apex tips, instead using the complex signal from a multi-apex probe as a rich dataset to be deconvolved, thereby bridging the gap between tip characterization and heterogeneous material property measurement.

proposals.title: Closed-Loop Quantum Device Fabrication via GW-Screened Tip-Nanotube Interactions
proposals.motivation: The deterministic assembly of atomic-scale quantum devices from carbon nanotubes (CNTs) remains a major challenge, limited by stochastic tip-atom interactions and poor understanding of the local dielectric screening. This impedes the reliable creation of devices like quantum dots or single-defect transistors, which are critical for quantum information and sensing. A predictive, first-principles framework linking tip-induced forces to the atomically screened CNT response is needed to move from serendipitous manipulation to precise engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The GW method can provide an accurate, material-specific dielectric function for a target CNT, enabling the ab initio design of voltage pulses for a characterized scanning probe tip that allow for deterministic atomic manipulation. The quantum transport properties of the resulting device will be predictable within the same GW-based electronic structure framework.
proposals.method: We will first use the GW-Bethe-Salpeter equation (GW-BSE) to compute the spatially resolved, frequency-dependent dielectric function of specific (n,m) CNTs. This will be integrated into a real-space electrostatic model of the tip-CNT junction, using tip profiles characterized via field emission resonance. We will then employ time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) with the GW-derived screening to simulate and optimize voltage pulse sequences for atom/defect manipulation. Finally, fabricated devices will be measured in-situ for quantum transport (e.g., conductance, Coulomb blockade) and compared to transport calculations using the GW-corrected electronic band structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a closed-loop, first-principles platform for atomically precise quantum device fabrication. It will deliver a validated protocol for deterministic atomic manipulation on CNTs and provide fundamental insights into the role of dynamical screening in tip-induced chemical processes. The direct comparison of predicted and measured quantum transport will set a new standard for ab initio device design.
proposals.novelty: The integration of GW-computed, non-local dielectric screening into the real-space design of scanning probe manipulation pulses is a novel synthesis of many-body theory and atomic-scale engineering. This moves beyond standard density functional theory (DFT) or classical models, which fail to accurately capture the critical screened interactions in low-dimensional systems.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Empirical-Tight-Binding Framework for Predictive Simulation of Droop-Free Fin-LEDs
proposals.motivation: The development of droop-free Fin-LEDs, as demonstrated in recent work, promises revolutionary high-brightness, high-current light sources. However, optimizing their performance requires understanding the intricate link between nanoscale morphology, strain, and electronic properties like band structure and carrier transport. Existing methods either lack electronic detail (empirical potentials) or are computationally prohibitive for large, complex 3D nanostructures (full ab initio).
proposals.hypothesis: By hybridizing a Tersoff-type empirical potential for structural relaxation with a parameterized tight-binding model for electronic states, we can create an efficient, predictive simulation tool that accurately captures the influence of facet-specific strain and contact geometry on the electronic properties of III-nitride Fin-LEDs.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-scale framework where atomic coordinates and strain are first relaxed using a Tersoff potential parameterized for III-nitrides (e.g., GaN, InGaN). The resulting atomic positions will serve as direct input for a nearest-neighbor sp3d5s* tight-binding Hamiltonian, with on-site and hopping parameters adjusted for local strain via deformation potential theory. The electronic structure (band edges, local density of states) and carrier wavefunctions will be solved using the Lanczos algorithm for the large, sparse Hamiltonian. This will be implemented for full 3D Fin-LED geometries, focusing on facet-dependent properties near selective contacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomistic, electronic-structure-based explanation for the superior droop performance of Fin-LEDs, linking specific facet engineering to reduced Auger recombination and improved carrier injection. It will deliver an open-source computational tool that bridges structural and electronic simulation for semiconductor nanostructures, enabling rapid design of next-generation high-efficiency optoelectronic devices.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid quantum-mechanical/molecular-mechanical (QM/MM) methods exist, applying a tight-binding/empirical-potential hybrid specifically to the complex 3D morphology of facet-engineered Fin-LEDs is novel. This approach uniquely targets the critical mesoscale where device geometry dictates electronic performance, a regime poorly served by pure DFT or continuum models.

proposals.title: Direct Bandgap Emission from Ligand-Stabilized Silicon Nanoclusters: A Pathway to Silicon-Based Droop-Free LEDs
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of conventional III-nitride LEDs suffers from efficiency droop at high current densities, limiting brightness. Silicon is an ideal, abundant, and CMOS-compatible material, but bulk silicon is an indirect bandgap semiconductor, making it a poor light emitter. Understanding and engineering the electronic properties of very small silicon clusters could unlock a new class of efficient, droop-free light sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that silicon clusters below a critical size (e.g., Si_n, n<~50 atoms), when appropriately surface-passivated, will exhibit a direct or quasi-direct bandgap due to quantum confinement and symmetry breaking, enabling efficient, droop-free electroluminescence.
proposals.method: We will synthesize atomically precise silicon clusters (Si_n, n=10-100) via solution-phase chemistry, using organic ligands (e.g., phenylacetylene) for stabilization and surface electronic control. Their electronic structure will be calculated using density functional theory (DFT) with hybrid functionals (HSE06) and GW corrections to accurately predict bandgaps and optical transition strengths. Promising candidates will be integrated into prototype LED devices using facet-selective contact strategies, akin to recent fin-LED architectures, to enable efficient carrier injection into the nanoscale active regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental structure-property map linking silicon cluster size and surface chemistry to direct bandgap behavior. It will demonstrate a proof-of-concept silicon-based LED, challenging the paradigm that silicon cannot be the active emitter in efficient optoelectronics and potentially enabling a new materials platform for high-brightness, droop-free micro-LEDs.
proposals.novelty: While quantum confinement in silicon nanocrystals is known, the systematic exploration of the sub-nanocrystal, molecular cluster regime for direct bandgap engineering is unexplored. Combining this with advanced, droop-mitigating device architectures (facet-selective contacts) represents a novel, synergistic materials-and-device approach.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Origins of Efficiency Droop in High-Brightness LEDs: A Comparative Study of Contamination, Geometry, and Electronic Structure
proposals.motivation: Efficiency droop at high current densities remains a critical bottleneck for the brightness and power scaling of III-nitride and perovskite LEDs. The dominant pre-2026 explanation centers on electronic structure effects like Auger recombination or carrier leakage due to weak electron-photon coupling. However, recent advances in droop-free fin-LEDs suggest device geometry and material quality may play a more decisive role than previously assumed, challenging the established paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for state-of-the-art, high-current LED architectures, extrinsic factors—specifically, facet-dependent surface contamination and the three-dimensional carrier/photon confinement geometry—are the primary determinants of droop, overshadowing the intrinsic limitations of band structure and weak coupling.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a matrix of GaN-based fin-LEDs and conventional planar LEDs with controlled variations in facet orientation, surface passivation, and intentional trace metal contamination. Using cathodoluminescence and atom probe tomography, we will correlate local non-radiative recombination centers with operational metrics. We will employ fully-coupled 3D electro-optical simulations, solving drift-diffusion and photon rate equations, to deconvolve the contributions of geometry-induced current crowding, surface recombination velocity, and Auger coefficients extracted from temperature-dependent photoluminescence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, experimentally-grounded ranking of droop mechanisms in modern LED designs. It will establish fabrication guidelines—focused on facet engineering and contamination control—to mitigate droop, directly informing the development of next-generation high-brightness micro-LEDs and lasers. The findings could shift the research focus from fundamental band structure engineering towards practical device architecture and processing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work isolates individual droop factors, this study directly tests their relative dominance by co-varying contamination, geometry, and electronic structure in a single experimental framework. It leverages the unique facet-dependent properties of fin-LEDs, a pre-2026 innovation, as a tool to probe the fundamental limits of light emission.

proposals.title: Strong Fermi-Level Pinning via Orbital-Resolved Hybridization for Droop-Free High-Brightness Fin-LEDs
proposals.motivation: In high-current-density light-emitting diodes (LEDs), especially the novel fin-LED architecture, efficiency droop remains a critical limitation to achieving ultimate brightness. A primary contributor to droop is carrier leakage, which is exacerbated by inefficient carrier injection and poor Fermi-level alignment at the metal-semiconductor contacts. Strong, tunable Fermi surface coupling (pinning) at these interfaces could fundamentally suppress leakage by ensuring optimal band alignment under high injection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific combinations of transition metals and III-nitride semiconductor surfaces, selected for their orbital symmetry and energy matching, can create strong, chemically stable Fermi-level pinning, thereby enabling droop-free operation in fin-LEDs at ultra-high current densities.
proposals.method: We will employ first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) to screen candidate metal (e.g., Ni, Pd, Pt, W) and nitride surface (GaN, AlN, InGaN) combinations. The screening will focus on the orbital-resolved density of states and the charge transfer at the interface to quantify the pinning strength (S parameter). Promising material pairs will then be fabricated into facet-selective contacts on prototype fin-LED structures using ultra-high-vacuum electron-beam deposition, with electrical (IV, CV) and optical (light-output-current) characterization used to validate droop suppression.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials design principle for strong Fermi surface coupling in nitride optoelectronics. It will directly contribute to the realization of practical, droop-free fin-LEDs for high-brightness applications such as micro-displays and visible-light communication, moving beyond the empirical optimization of contacts.
proposals.novelty: While Fermi-level pinning is a known phenomenon, proactively designing for *strong and beneficial* pinning via orbital hybridization in the context of the novel fin-LED geometry—where facet-selective contacts are critical—is a novel and non-obvious materials-centric strategy to solve efficiency droop.

proposals.title: Non-Equilibrium Tunneling and Carrier Dynamics in Tip-Induced Localized States for High-Brightness LEDs
proposals.motivation: In high-current-density LEDs, efficiency droop remains a critical bottleneck for achieving ultimate brightness. Recent work on droop-free fin LEDs suggests that managing carrier injection and distribution is key. A fundamental, unresolved aspect is the role of non-equilibrium electronic populations in localized states, such as those induced by nanoscale contacts or defects, which could dominate transport and recombination at high drive currents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the tunneling current in semiconductor devices under high injection is governed by a non-Fermi-Dirac population of carriers in tip-induced or defect-localized states, and that modeling this non-equilibrium distribution is essential for accurately predicting and mitigating efficiency loss at high brightness.
proposals.method: We will develop a kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) model coupled with a non-equilibrium Green's function (NEGF) formalism to simulate carrier injection and transport. The model will explicitly treat the population of tip-induced localized states (TILS) using a master equation approach, tracking occupation probabilities beyond equilibrium statistics. Parameters for the TILS (energy, capture/emission rates) will be derived from first-principles DFT calculations on relevant III-nitride or perovskite surfaces, informed by Tersoff's recent materials work. The simulation will be validated against current-voltage and electroluminescence data from state-of-the-art fin LED structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles-linked simulation framework to quantify the impact of non-equilibrium localized states on high-current transport. It will directly inform the design of facet-selective contacts and active regions in droop-free LEDs by identifying the specific electronic conditions that lead to efficiency saturation. The resulting insights will bridge atomic-scale defect physics to macroscopic device performance.
proposals.novelty: While NEGF is standard for tunneling, its application to explicitly model non-thermal carrier distributions in experimentally relevant tip-induced states for high-brightness LEDs is novel. This moves beyond standard quasi-equilibrium approximations to address the core of the high-injection problem.

proposals.title: Generalizing Tip-Induced Localized States: A Tight-Binding Framework for Metallic and Semiconducting Surfaces
proposals.motivation: The theory of Tip-Induced Localized States (TILS) has successfully explained anomalous scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) conductance features on graphite surfaces, linking them to localized electronic states under the tip. However, its application has been largely confined to graphite, a semi-metal with a unique band structure. Extending this framework to broader material classes—especially conventional metals and technologically relevant semiconductors—is crucial for quantitatively interpreting STM data on surfaces critical for catalysis, electronics, and light-emitting devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the tight-binding model underpinning TILS can be systematically generalized to predict and engineer localized states on metallic (e.g., Au, Cu) and semiconducting (e.g., GaN, transition metal dichalcogenide) surfaces, with the state's energy and spatial extent controlled by the tip material's work function and the surface's electronic density of states.
proposals.method: We will employ a parameterized tight-binding model, expanding the Hamiltonian to include realistic surface band structures from pre-2026 DFT databases (e.g., Materials Project). The tip will be modeled as a perturbation with a potential profile derived from its known electronic properties. We will develop numerical codes to solve the eigenvalue problem for this coupled tip-surface system, calculating local density of states maps. Specific validation will involve simulating STM on Au(111) (known surface state) and GaN (relevant to LED research), comparing results to published experimental STS data from pre-2026 literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified theoretical tool for the STM community to deconvolve tip effects from intrinsic surface electronic structure, reducing interpretation errors. It will establish design principles for selecting tip materials to selectively probe or even manipulate localized states on target surfaces, with direct implications for characterizing defects and heterostructures in optoelectronic materials like those used in high-brightness LEDs.
proposals.novelty: While TILS is established for graphite, its deliberate extension to engineer states on metals and semiconductors by tailoring the tip material is novel. This moves the theory from a descriptive tool for an anomaly to a predictive framework for surface state engineering.

proposals.title: Surface Green's Function Modeling of Facet-Selective Contacts for Droop-Free Fin LEDs
proposals.motivation: The recent development of droop-free fin LEDs using facet-selective contacts is a breakthrough for high-brightness, high-current operation. However, the fundamental physics of the tunneling junction at these nanoscale semiconductor-metal interfaces is not fully understood, limiting predictive design. Efficient, atomistically-informed models for these contacts are needed to optimize carrier injection and further push the limits of LED efficiency and lasing thresholds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the non-equilibrium Green's function (NEGF) formalism, adapted from surface science for transport, can accurately and efficiently model the quantum tunneling and band alignment at the facet-selective contacts in fin LEDs. This approach will reveal the dominant mechanisms governing high-current, droop-free injection.
proposals.method: We will construct an atomistic tight-binding model of the fin LED's active region and contact interfaces, based on pre-2026 experimental structures. The surface Green's function technique, specifically the Sancho-Rubio iterative method, will be employed to calculate the contact self-energies and electronic density of states efficiently for the complex facet geometries. This will be integrated into an NEGF transport solver to compute the current-voltage characteristics and local carrier densities under high injection, benchmarking against existing experimental data on chip-scale fin LEDs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles-based computational tool specifically tailored for designing facet-engineered contacts in III-nitride LEDs. It will deliver a quantitative understanding of the tunneling physics enabling droop-free operation, guiding the material and geometric optimization of next-generation ultra-bright light sources and on-chip lasers.
proposals.novelty: While NEGF is used in nanoelectronics, its application to the specific problem of facet-selective contacts in III-nitride fin LEDs is novel. The integration of surface Green's function techniques for efficient contact modeling within the context of high-current optoelectronic device optimization represents a significant methodological advance over standard drift-diffusion or simplified tunneling models.

proposals.title: Atomic-Scale Control of Charge Injection in Fin-LEDs via Engineered Tip Impurity States
proposals.motivation: In high-brightness, droop-free fin LEDs, the efficiency at high current density is critically limited by non-radiative recombination at metal-semiconductor contacts and carrier injection bottlenecks. Pre-2026 understanding of these interfaces is largely macroscopic, obscuring the role of atomic-scale disorder. Treating the contact apex as a single adsorbed impurity provides a fundamental model to understand and ultimately control the electronic states that govern injection.
proposals.hypothesis: The electronic states induced by a single, well-defined impurity at a semiconductor contact interface can be engineered to create a resonant tunneling pathway, dramatically reducing the Schottky barrier and associated Joule heating for high-current operation.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) with hybrid functionals (e.g., HSE06) to model the electronic band structure of III-nitride fin surfaces with a single adsorbed atom (e.g., Si, Mg, O) at the tip apex. Non-equilibrium Green's function (NEGF) calculations will be integrated to simulate ballistic electron transport and local density of states under high bias, mimicking operational LED conditions. The system will be validated against scanning tunneling spectroscopy data from collaborative experiments on model surfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-principles, atomic-scale framework for understanding charge injection in nanoscale LEDs, directly linking impurity states to device-level metrics like differential resistance and droop. It will provide specific design rules for facet-selective contact engineering to optimize high-current performance.
proposals.novelty: While impurity doping is well-studied in bulk semiconductors, applying the single-impurity model to a functional device contact interface—specifically the apex of a fin LED—to engineer injection is a novel, bottom-up approach distinct from traditional contact metallurgy studies.

proposals.title: Predicting Droop-Free High-Brightness LED Performance from First-Principles Superlattice Self-Organization
proposals.motivation: Achieving high brightness and efficiency in LEDs at high current densities is limited by efficiency droop, a fundamental materials challenge. Recent advances in facet-selective contacts and fin-LED architectures have demonstrated droop-free operation, but their design relies on empirical optimization. A predictive, multiscale framework connecting the atomic-scale self-organization of materials (like perovskites or III-nitride superlattices) to macroscopic device properties would revolutionize the design of next-generation optoelectronics.
proposals.hypothesis: The macroscopic current-brightness characteristics and droop behavior of a fin-LED are directly determined by the microscopic electronic band structure and carrier dynamics, which are emergent properties of the self-organized superlattice within the active region.
proposals.method: We will employ a hierarchical computational approach. First, density functional theory (DFT) and kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) simulations will model the self-assembly and electronic band structure of candidate superlattices (e.g., perovskite or InGaN/GaN). These results will parameterize a drift-diffusion model, enhanced with a microscopic description of radiative and non-radiative recombination rates (e.g., using ABC coefficients derived from first principles). This multiscale model will be implemented in a TCAD-like framework (e.g., using Silvaco Atlas or a custom solver) to simulate the full fin-LED device geometry, predicting I-V and light-output curves.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-principles-to-device simulation pipeline, moving beyond phenomenological models. It will provide specific design rules for superlattice composition and fin geometry to maximize brightness and minimize droop, directly guiding experimental synthesis of high-performance LEDs.
proposals.novelty: While multiscale modeling exists, directly linking the kinetic self-organization process of soft/hard superlattices to the performance metrics of a proven, non-planar device architecture (the droop-free fin-LED) is a novel and powerful integration of materials physics and device engineering.

proposals.title: Electrostatic Doping and Carrier Dynamics in Nanotube-Based Fin-LEDs for Droop-Free High-Brightness Emission
proposals.motivation: Achieving high-brightness, droop-free light emission at high current densities remains a critical challenge for solid-state lighting and lasing. Recent work on fin-LEDs has shown promise by using geometric design to mitigate efficiency droop, but these studies typically assume intrinsic semiconductor channels. Controlling carrier injection and density via electrostatic doping in low-dimensional channels like nanotubes could provide a new lever to manage Auger recombination and carrier overflow, the primary causes of droop.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that electrostatic gating of a nanotube channel within a fin-LED architecture will enable precise, dynamic control over carrier density and type, allowing for the suppression of efficiency droop at ultra-high current densities by optimizing the balance between electron and hole populations.
proposals.method: We will fabricate fin-LEDs using single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) or selected semiconductor nanotubes as the active channel, integrated with facet-selective metal contacts as demonstrated in recent chip-scale droop-free LEDs. A global back-gate or patterned local gates will be used to electrostatically dope the nanotube channel from p-type to n-type. Device performance will be characterized by measuring light output power, external quantum efficiency (EQE), and electroluminescence spectra as a function of gate voltage and injection current density. Band structure modeling and rate equation analysis will be employed to correlate the gate-tuned density of states and Fermi level with the observed carrier recombination dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational understanding of how electrostatic doping influences efficiency droop in low-dimensional light emitters. It will provide design rules for next-generation, voltage-tunable high-brightness LEDs and lasers, potentially enabling devices whose emission wavelength and efficiency can be dynamically optimized for different operating conditions.
proposals.novelty: While gated nanotubes have been studied for transistors, their application as the actively doped channel in a high-current, high-brightness fin-LED architecture to combat efficiency droop is a novel and unexplored synthesis of concepts from nanoelectronics and photonics.

proposals.title: Impact of Chirality Dispersion and Atomic Defects on Current Injection, Droop, and Brightness in Carbon Nanotube-Based Light-Emitting Diodes
proposals.motivation: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are promising emitters for high-brightness, nanoscale LEDs due to their direct bandgap tunability with chirality. However, realistic CNT syntheses produce mixtures of chiralities and inherent atomic defects, which are often idealized away in device models. This gap obscures the true limits of CNT-LED performance, particularly regarding efficiency droop at high current densities—a critical challenge in solid-state lighting that Dr. Tersoff's recent work on droop-free Fin LEDs directly addresses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific chiralities and common defect types (e.g., Stone-Wales, vacancies) will create localized electronic states that act as non-radiative recombination centers or current leakage paths, fundamentally limiting the maximum achievable brightness and exacerbating efficiency droop in CNT-LEDs under high injection.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-scale computational approach. First, density functional theory (DFT) calculations will characterize the electronic structure and defect formation energies for a range of semiconducting chiralities (e.g., (n,m) = (6,5), (8,7), (10,5)). Second, non-equilibrium Green's function (NEGF) simulations, parameterized with DFT results, will model carrier transport and recombination in CNT p-i-n junctions with explicit defects. This will be coupled with a rate equation model for high-current operation to quantify radiative vs. non-radiative pathways and predict current-brightness characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative map linking specific synthesis-induced imperfections to key LED performance metrics like external quantum efficiency and droop. It will guide experimental efforts by identifying which chiralities and defect densities are most detrimental, informing targeted purification or passivation strategies to push CNT-LEDs toward the droop-free, high-brightness operation demonstrated in other material systems.
proposals.novelty: While CNT electronic structure is well-studied, systematically connecting realistic structural disorder to the high-current operational physics of LEDs—specifically efficiency droop—is novel. This bridges the materials-focused defect science of CNTs with the device-level performance challenges central to Dr. Tersoff's recent research on advanced LED architectures.

proposals.title: Direct Experimental Mapping of Source-Drain Tunneling Current in III-Nitride Fin LEDs as a Function of Channel Length Down to 5 nm
proposals.motivation: As semiconductor devices approach the sub-10 nm scale, source-drain tunneling is predicted to impose a fundamental limit on electrostatic control and efficiency, degrading performance in transistors and LEDs. However, precise experimental validation of this scaling limit in high-current, light-emitting devices is lacking, creating uncertainty for future miniaturization of photonic and electronic components. This work directly addresses a critical knowledge gap in the ultimate scaling of optoelectronic devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for III-nitride-based fin LED structures with facet-selective contacts, a distinct and measurable collapse in internal quantum efficiency (IQE) and a change in current-voltage characteristics will occur at channel lengths below 10 nm, dominated by non-radiative tunneling currents rather than traditional efficiency droop mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of droop-free fin LEDs, leveraging established facet-selective contact techniques to define the conductive channel length lithographically and via controlled etching. Channel lengths will be systematically varied from 20 nm down to 5 nm. Electrical characterization (I-V, differential conductance) will be coupled with precise optical power and efficiency measurements (light-output vs. current, L-I) at cryogenic and room temperatures to separate tunneling currents from thermionic emission and quantify their impact on efficiency. The experimental data will be compared with Wentzel–Kramers–Brillouin (WKB) and non-equilibrium Green's function (NEGF) simulations of tunneling in the fin geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clear experimental dataset mapping the onset of source-drain tunneling as a dominant loss mechanism in scaled light-emitting devices. It will establish a practical scaling limit for III-nitride fin LEDs and create a methodology for separating tunneling losses from other efficiency droop factors, informing the design of future ultra-dense optoelectronic integrated circuits.
proposals.novelty: While tunneling limits are well-studied theoretically for MOSFETs, applying this framework to high-brightness, droop-free fin LEDs—where the active region itself becomes the tunneling channel—is a novel and critical extension. The use of facet-selective contacts provides a uniquely clean platform to isolate the tunneling effect from parasitic resistances.

proposals.title: Direct Probing of Charge-State-Dependent Auger Recombination and Efficiency Droop in Nanotube-Embedded Fin LEDs
proposals.motivation: Efficiency droop at high current densities remains a critical bottleneck for high-brightness LEDs. While fin LED architectures and novel contact schemes have shown promise in mitigating droop, the fundamental role of charge carrier dynamics in low-dimensional nanostructures above threshold is not fully understood. Investigating how the charge state of embedded nanotubes influences non-radiative Auger recombination above threshold is essential for designing next-generation droop-free, high-current devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that above the lasing threshold, the specific charge population (e.g., trion vs. exciton dominance) within embedded nanotubes directly governs the Auger recombination rate, and that engineering the nanotube's electrostatic environment can suppress this loss pathway, enabling higher brightness and efficiency.
proposals.method: We will fabricate chip-scale fin LEDs with embedded single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) or related semiconductor nanotubes, using facet-selective contacts to enable high-current injection. Device behavior will be characterized by simultaneous electroluminescence and photoluminescence spectroscopy under pulsed high-current operation. We will employ gate-dependent spectroscopy to actively modulate the nanotube charge state and correlate it with changes in the light-output-versus-current (L-I) curve, specifically measuring the droop onset. Kinetic models will be fitted to the data to extract charge-state-dependent Auger coefficients.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between the tunable charge state of a nanoscale emitter and the macroscopic efficiency droop in a practical LED. It will provide design rules for charge management in nanostructured active regions to suppress Auger losses. The findings will advance the fundamental understanding of high-current limits in nanoscale light emitters and guide material integration for superior high-brightness devices.
proposals.novelty: While previous work on fin LEDs addressed droop via geometry and contacts, this proposal uniquely focuses on the *internal*, tunable charge state of the emitting nanostructure itself as a control knob for Auger recombination, a direct investigation not yet performed in an operational, high-current LED context.

proposals.title: Quantum Transport and Efficiency Droop in Nanoscale Fin LEDs: A Non-Equilibrium Green's Function Analysis
proposals.motivation: Achieving high brightness in sub-micrometer LEDs, as demonstrated in recent fin-LED architectures, is critically limited by efficiency droop at high current densities. Pre-2026 models often rely on semi-classical drift-diffusion or phenomenological ABC models, which fail to fully capture the quantum-confined transport and non-equilibrium carrier dynamics at the nanoscale. A first-principles quantum transport framework is needed to elucidate the fundamental limits and pathways for droop-free operation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the facet-selective contacts and extreme geometric confinement in fin-LEDs create non-equilibrium carrier distributions and modified local density of states that can suppress Auger recombination and mitigate efficiency droop, phenomena that can be rigorously captured and engineered using the NEGF formalism.
proposals.method: We will implement a 2D real-space NEGF solver coupled self-consistently with the Poisson equation to model carrier injection and transport in the fin-LED cross-section. The model will use a tight-binding Hamiltonian derived from the material's band structure (e.g., for GaN-based systems) to account for quantum confinement. Scattering mechanisms (electron-phonon, alloy disorder) will be incorporated via self-energies within the self-consistent Born approximation. The radiative and Auger recombination rates will be computed directly from the non-equilibrium lesser Green's function (G<).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully quantum-mechanical map of carrier density, potential, and recombination profiles within operating nanoscale fin-LEDs. It will identify the specific transport mechanisms that enable high current injection without droop, offering quantitative design rules for contact engineering and fin geometry. The developed computational framework will be a versatile tool for analyzing next-generation nanoscale optoelectronic devices.
proposals.novelty: While NEGF is standard in mesoscopic electron devices, its application to model high-current, high-brightness light emission in III-nitride fin-LEDs is novel. This approach moves beyond conventional rate equations to directly compute recombination from non-equilibrium quantum statistics in a realistically confined and contacted nanostructure.

proposals.title: Droop-Free, High-Brightness Light Emission from Carbon Nanotube Network LEDs via Facet-Selective Contact Engineering
proposals.motivation: Achieving high-brightness, droop-free light emission at high current densities remains a critical challenge for solid-state lighting and lasing, as seen in the efficiency collapse of conventional III-nitride LEDs. While recent work on fin LEDs demonstrates a path to droop-free operation via geometric and contact engineering, scaling to larger, manufacturable areas is difficult. Carbon nanotube (CNT) networks offer a promising alternative active material due to their solution processability and excellent electronic properties, but their performance in high-current, high-brightness electroluminescent devices is unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying the principles of facet-selective electrical injection, recently proven in fin LEDs, to a percolating network of semiconducting single-walled carbon nanotubes (s-SWCNTs), we can create a scalable, thin-film LED that exhibits droop-free, high-brightness electroluminescence at current densities exceeding 1 kA/cm².
proposals.method: We will fabricate planar LED devices using dense, purified s-SWCNT networks as the emissive layer. Key to the approach is the design of asymmetric, facet-selective contacts: one electrode (e.g., Pd) will form an ohmic contact for efficient hole injection across a large area, while the opposing electrode (e.g., Al/LiF) will be patterned into an array of nanoscale line contacts to locally inject electrons with high current density, mimicking the fin LED architecture. Device performance (light output power, external quantum efficiency, spectral stability) will be characterized as a function of current density and compared to control devices with standard symmetric contacts. Finite-element modeling of carrier injection and transport within the random CNT network will guide contact geometry optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first demonstration of droop-free electroluminescence from a solution-processed, thin-film CNT network, pushing their performance into the high-brightness regime relevant for lighting and gain media. It will provide a novel device architecture that decouples the requirements for high current injection from large-area fabrication, bridging the gap between nanoscale fin LEDs and scalable, macroscopic devices.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of the facet-selective contact concept—a cutting-edge method for combating efficiency droop in epitaxial semiconductors—with the unique electronic and optical properties of a random network of 1D nanomaterials, creating a new class of scalable, high-current light emitters.

proposals.title: Droop-Free Fin-LEDs with 2D h-BN Gate Dielectrics: Enabling High-Brightness, Low-Voltage Operation
proposals.motivation: The pursuit of high-brightness, droop-free LEDs for micro-displays and lasing is limited by the need for high current injection, which exacerbates efficiency droop and thermal issues. Scaling the gate oxide in fin-LED architectures is critical to reduce operating voltage and power consumption, but conventional SiO2 scaling is physically limited. Modern high-k materials and 2D insulators like hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) offer a path to superior electrostatic control with minimal gate leakage, directly addressing the voltage and efficiency challenges in high-current LED operation.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating an atomically thin, high-quality h-BN gate dielectric into the facet-selective contact architecture of droop-free fin-LEDs will significantly lower the threshold voltage for carrier injection while maintaining high current density and light output, thereby enhancing wall-plug efficiency at high brightness.
proposals.method: We will fabricate fin-LED structures using established selective-area growth techniques for III-nitrides. Atomically thin h-BN layers will be synthesized via CVD and transferred onto the fin sidewalls using a polymer-free, van der Waals dry transfer method to form the gate dielectric. Facet-selective contacts will be deposited via angled evaporation to create the gate electrode. Electrical characterization will focus on current-voltage (I-V) and capacitance-voltage (C-V) measurements to extract gate leakage, dielectric constant, and threshold voltage. Optical output power and efficiency will be measured as a function of current density and compared to control devices with conventional oxides.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel materials integration strategy for next-generation LED architectures, providing a clear pathway to lower operating voltages in high-current density devices. It will establish the performance benefits of 2D dielectrics in optoelectronic devices, contributing directly to the goal of efficient, high-brightness micro-light sources for displays and visible-light communication.
proposals.novelty: While 2D insulators are explored in transistors, their application to the specific facet-gated, high-current geometry of droop-free fin-LEDs is unexplored. This combines Tersoff's expertise in fin-LED design with emerging 2D material processing to solve a core voltage-scaling problem in optoelectronics.

proposals.title: Atomistic Modeling of Silicon Nanoclusters and Quantum Dots Using a Modified Tersoff Potential for Surface-Dominated Optoelectronic Properties
proposals.motivation: Silicon nanoclusters and quantum dots (QDs) are promising for next-generation light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and lasers due to their tunable bandgap and compatibility with silicon photonics. However, at these sub-5 nm scales, surface states and reconstructions dominate electronic and optical properties, leading to efficiency droop and unpredictable behavior. Existing empirical potentials often fail to accurately capture the surface chemistry and quantum confinement effects critical for predicting performance in high-brightness, droop-free light emission.
proposals.hypothesis: A modified Tersoff interatomic potential, parameterized using first-principles data on silicon surface reconstructions and passivation, can accurately model the structural, electronic, and thermal properties of silicon nanoclusters and QDs, providing a computationally efficient bridge between ab initio methods and device-scale models.
proposals.method: We will develop a modified Tersoff potential by incorporating environment-dependent bond-order terms and explicit surface energy corrections, fitted to density functional theory (DFT) calculations of hydrogen- and oxygen-passivated Si nanoclusters. This potential will be implemented in LAMMPS for large-scale molecular dynamics simulations of cluster formation and thermal stability. Electronic structure analysis, including approximate band edges and density of states, will be extracted using the local atomic configurations from the simulations via a tight-binding post-processing scheme validated against DFT.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, efficient computational tool for designing surface-engineered silicon QDs with suppressed non-radiative recombination, directly addressing the droop challenge in nanoscale LEDs. It will establish a clear atomistic structure-property relationship for quantum-confined silicon systems, guiding experimental synthesis for high-brightness, chip-scale light sources.
proposals.novelty: While the Tersoff potential is standard for bulk silicon, its application and specific modification to model the surface-dominated optoelectronic properties of nanoclusters and quantum dots is novel. This approach leverages the efficiency of classical potentials to access the size and time scales relevant to synthesis and degradation, which are prohibitive for pure DFT studies.

proposals.title: Many-Body Coulomb Interactions in Droop-Free Fin LEDs: A Self-Consistent Tight-Binding Approach
proposals.motivation: The development of droop-free Fin LEDs has enabled unprecedented current densities and brightness, pushing devices into regimes where high carrier densities make electron-electron (e-e) interactions significant. Pre-2026 models for these devices, including tight-binding methods, typically neglect these many-body effects, which can critically alter band structures, carrier distributions, and recombination dynamics. A predictive model for next-generation high-brightness LEDs requires moving beyond the independent-electron approximation to account for Coulomb interactions under extreme injection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating screened Coulomb interactions self-consistently into a tight-binding framework will reveal a significant renormalization of the electronic band structure and carrier confinement in Fin LEDs at high current densities, providing a fundamental explanation for their superior efficiency droop suppression compared to conventional designs.
proposals.method: We will develop a parameterized tight-binding model for the Fin LED active region, based on pre-2026 materials data. Electron-electron interactions will be included via a self-consistent Hartree-Fock approximation, using a statically screened Coulomb potential (e.g., Yukawa-type) appropriate for the high-density plasma. The method will be implemented using a custom real-space Green's function solver, enabling calculation of the renormalized band edges, quasi-Fermi levels, and wavefunction overlap integrals as a function of injected current density. Key outputs will be the current-dependent radiative recombination coefficient and its comparison to droop-prone structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, many-body electronic structure model for the operational state of droop-free Fin LEDs, directly linking microscopic Coulomb interactions to macroscopic efficiency metrics. It will establish a new computational framework for designing ultra-bright LEDs and nanoscale lasers by accurately predicting performance limits at ultra-high current densities. The results will guide material and contact engineering to further mitigate non-radiative losses.
proposals.novelty: While many-body corrections are studied in bulk materials, their self-consistent integration into a device-scale tight-binding model for a cutting-edge, high-current LED architecture is novel. This approach uniquely targets the specific facet-selective contact and nanoscale geometry of the Fin LED, moving beyond phenomenological ABC models to a first-principles-inspired device physics description.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Explicit-Continuum Model for Dielectric Screening in Nanotube-Based LEDs: Quantifying Interface Effects on Droop and Brightness
proposals.motivation: Achieving high brightness in LEDs at high current densities is limited by efficiency droop, where non-radiative Auger recombination dominates. For nanostructured LEDs, like the fin LEDs in your recent work, the local dielectric environment at the semiconductor interface critically modifies exciton binding energies and charge carrier dynamics, but is poorly described by bulk dielectric constants. A predictive, multiscale model of this interface screening is needed to design materials and contacts that suppress droop.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the specific molecular configuration and dynamics at a nanotube or nanostructured semiconductor interface create a spatially and frequency-dependent dielectric screening profile that significantly reduces exciton binding energies and alters Auger recombination rates compared to predictions from a uniform dielectric model.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid quantum-classical-continuum framework. The core nanotube and its first adsorbed molecular layer (e.g., ligands, solvent) will be treated with density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT for ground and excited states. This explicit region will be embedded in a polarizable molecular mechanics (MM) layer using a force field like AMOEBA. The bulk environment will be modeled as a frequency-dependent dielectric continuum using a boundary element method (BEM) solver, self-consistently coupled to the QM/MM region via the Poisson equation. Charge transfer rates will be computed using Marcus theory with parameters from the model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, atomistically informed map of dielectric screening at complex nanostructured interfaces relevant to LEDs. It will directly predict how interface engineering—through specific molecular passivation or contact materials—can tune optical spectra and key non-radiative loss coefficients. The model will deliver design principles for droop-mitigating interfaces in high-current nanoscale optoelectronics.
proposals.novelty: While continuum dielectric models are standard, and QM/MM methods exist for biomolecules, their integration for predicting optoelectronic properties of synthetic nanostructures, with explicit treatment of frequency-dependent screening and its link to Auger processes, is novel. This moves beyond treating the environment as a static dielectric constant.

proposals.title: Dielectric Engineering of Exciton Dynamics and Carrier Transport in Nanostructured LEDs
proposals.motivation: The performance of nanostructured light-emitting diodes (LEDs), such as the droop-free fin LEDs recently demonstrated, is fundamentally governed by exciton binding energies, radiative recombination rates, and carrier injection efficiency. These parameters are strongly modified by dielectric screening from the surrounding environment (e.g., solvents, substrates, or encapsulation layers), an effect often treated simplistically. A quantitative model is needed to guide material and device design for optimal brightness and efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the non-local dielectric screening from solvents and substrates, we can predict and engineer key optoelectronic properties—specifically, the exciton binding energy and the effective band offsets at heterointerfaces—in semiconductor nanotubes and nanofins, leading to designs for higher brightness and lower efficiency droop.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-scale computational framework. First, we will use density functional theory (DFT) with van der Waals corrections to model the atomic structure of lead-halide perovskite and III-nitride nanorods/fins. The electrostatic problem of the nanostructure embedded in a dielectric continuum will then be solved using a real-space Poisson solver with appropriate boundary conditions to compute the screened Coulomb potential. This will feed into a many-body perturbation theory (GW-BSE) calculation to obtain accurate quasiparticle band gaps and excitonic properties as a function of the external dielectric constant.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive design tool for selecting optimal dielectric environments for nanostructured LEDs. It will deliver quantitative guidelines for maximizing radiative efficiency by tuning exciton binding and minimizing non-radiative Auger recombination through dielectric management of carrier densities. The results will directly inform experimental efforts in chip-scale, high-brightness light sources.
proposals.novelty: While dielectric screening is known conceptually, its quantitative, atomistically-informed integration into the design of real, high-current-density fin LED architectures is novel. This moves beyond bulk or planar models to address the unique geometry and facet-specific contact problems central to recent droop-free LED demonstrations.

proposals.title: Mapping Substrate Screening with Nanoscale Resolution Using Metallic Carbon Nanotubes as One-Dimensional Dielectric Probes
proposals.motivation: The performance of nanoscale electronic and optoelectronic devices, such as high-brightness LEDs, is critically dependent on the local dielectric environment and charge screening provided by the substrate. Pre-2026, understanding these substrate effects, especially on complex reconstructed or adsorbate-covered surfaces, has been limited by a lack of probes with sufficient spatial resolution and sensitivity to one-dimensional electronic states. Metallic carbon nanotubes (m-CNTs) offer a unique platform for this, as their low-energy plasmons and dielectric response are exquisitely sensitive to external potentials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a metallic carbon nanotube, when deposited on a substrate with a spatially varying surface potential, will act as a sensitive, one-dimensional probe whose modified dielectric function and plasmon dispersion directly map the local substrate-induced screening and charge redistribution with nanoscale axial resolution.
proposals.method: We will employ a unified computational framework combining a Slater-Koster tight-binding model for the m-CNT and substrate band structures with a self-consistent Hartree-Fock calculation to treat electron-electron correlations. The substrate's complex potential will be incorporated via a position-dependent dielectric screening function ε(r) derived from first-principles DFT for surfaces like Si(111)-7x7 or graphene on MoS2. We will specifically calculate the nanotube's modified low-energy plasmon dispersion using the random phase approximation (RPA) and compare it with analytical models for a one-dimensional electron gas on a heterogeneous dielectric background. Experimental validation will involve correlating Raman G-band shifts and near-field optical microscopy of plasmon modes with substrate topography.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new methodology for quantitatively mapping substrate dielectric properties with nanoscale resolution, directly informing the design of substrates for high-current, droop-free nanoscale LEDs and lasers. It will provide the first coupled analysis of how a complex substrate's electronic structure modifies the fundamental plasmonic response of a one-dimensional metallic system.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synergy lies in using the m-CNT not just as a device element but as an active probe; its intrinsic electronic response becomes a direct reporter on the substrate's local properties, enabling a two-way, spatially resolved analysis of dielectric screening that conventional scanning probes cannot achieve.

proposals.title: Chiral Nanotubes as Electromagnetic Helices: A Theoretical Framework for Anisotropic Dielectric Response and Optical Activity
proposals.motivation: Chiral semiconducting nanotubes exhibit unique optical properties, including circular dichroism and anisotropic light-matter interactions, crucial for next-generation photonic and optoelectronic devices like LEDs and lasers. However, existing models, often limited to zigzag or armchair configurations, fail to fully capture the complex, frequency-dependent dielectric tensor arising from the helical atomic lattice. A predictive, first-principles framework for the dielectric function of general chiral nanotubes is needed to engineer materials for high-brightness, polarization-controlled light emission.
proposals.hypothesis: The electronic polarization response of a chiral nanotube can be modeled by adapting helical antenna theory, where the atomic lattice is treated as a nanoscale electromagnetic helix, allowing its full dielectric tensor to be decomposed into axial and circumferential current modes.
proposals.method: We will develop a modified tight-binding model, parameterized from DFT calculations, to compute the nanotube's electronic structure. The optical matrix elements will be decomposed into components parallel and perpendicular to the tube axis and helical path, analogous to the current modes in a helical antenna. This decomposition will feed into a Kubo-Greenwood formalism to calculate the frequency-dependent, anisotropic dielectric tensor. The model will be validated against existing spectroscopic ellipsometry and circular dichroism data for carbon and boron nitride nanotubes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a closed-form theoretical framework to predict the complete dielectric response of any chiral nanotube, directly linking atomic structure to macroscopic optical constants. It will enable the rational design of nanotube-based materials for chiral photonics, low-droop LEDs exploiting anisotropic gain, and enhanced light extraction in nanoscale emitters.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct conceptual and mathematical analogy from macroscopic RF helical antenna theory to nanoscale electronic polarization, a cross-disciplinary leap not previously applied to model the fundamental dielectric properties of chiral nanotubes.

proposals.title: Dynamic Tip-Chiral Nanotube Dielectric Coupling: A Multiscale Model for Scanning Probe Microscopy Artifacts
proposals.motivation: Scanning probe microscopy (SPM) of chiral semiconducting nanotubes, such as single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) or emerging chiral perovskite nanostructures, often yields ambiguous or irreproducible data on local dielectric and electronic properties. Pre-2026, static models of the tip or sample fail to explain chirality-dependent contrast and scan hysteresis, which hinders the reliable extraction of critical parameters like local band structure and dielectric response for high-brightness LED applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamic, chirality-dependent dielectric tensor of a nanotube, when coupled to the evolving geometry and charge state of a scanning tip, creates a feedback loop that dictates SPM contrast and is the primary source of measurement artifacts and hysteresis.
proposals.method: We will develop a tightly coupled multiscale framework. First-principles DFT (using VASP or Quantum ESPRESSO) will calculate the anisotropic, frequency-dependent dielectric tensor for nanotubes of defined (n,m) chirality. These tensors will drive a finite-element electrostatic solver within a molecular dynamics (MD, using LAMMPS with ReaxFF) simulation of a metallic tip apex during a scan. Charge redistribution on the tip will be modeled via a density functional tight-binding (DFTB) module, and the coupled tip-sample force and tunneling current will be computed at each step using a modified Bardeen approach, iterating the electrostatic boundary conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first predictive model for chirality-specific SPM artifacts, enabling more accurate interpretation of nanoscale dielectric and electronic maps. It will deliver a computational tool to deconvolve intrinsic nanotube properties from tip-induced perturbations, directly benefiting the characterization of nanomaterials for light-emitting devices where local electronic structure dictates performance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the real-time, two-way coupling between a dynamically evolving tip and a chirality-dependent anisotropic dielectric object, moving beyond the standard practice of treating either the tip or the sample as a static perturbation in SPM simulation.

proposals.title: Droop-Free Brightness in Nanotube LEDs via a 1D-Specific GW Formalism for Accurate Quasiparticle and Screening Predictions
proposals.motivation: Achieving high brightness and efficiency in nanoscale LEDs, such as the fin LEDs in your recent work, is limited by efficiency droop and inaccurate predictions of band gaps and screening in low-dimensional materials. Standard GW methods, designed for 3D bulk systems, fail for quasi-1D structures like carbon nanotubes due to artificial long-range Coulomb interactions from periodic boundary conditions and improper treatment of anisotropic screening, leading to unreliable predictions for device design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a GW approximation with a 1D-tailored basis set and a truncated Coulomb interaction will eliminate spurious periodic interactions and correctly capture anisotropic screening, enabling the first efficient and accurate first-principles calculation of quasiparticle band gaps and dielectric functions for carbon nanotubes of arbitrary chirality.
proposals.method: We will develop a new computational code that implements the GW approximation on a real-space grid tailored for cylindrical symmetry. A localized, atom-centered basis set optimized for 1D periodicity will be used. The key innovation is the implementation of a truncated Coulomb interaction that decays to zero beyond a cutoff radius in the non-periodic directions, removing artificial image interactions. We will compute the dielectric matrix and self-energy within this formalism, benchmarking against known experimental data for band gaps of specific (n,m) nanotubes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, first-principles platform to predict the fundamental electronic and optical properties of any carbon nanotube, directly informing the design of nanotube-based LEDs and lasers. It will establish the quantitative relationship between nanotube structure, quasiparticle band gap, and screening, which is critical for engineering high-current, high-brightness devices without efficiency droop.
proposals.novelty: While truncated Coulomb interactions exist for 2D materials, a fully integrated 1D-specific GW formalism with a tailored basis set for nanotubes is novel. This directly addresses the core computational pathology in simulating 1D systems, moving beyond ad-hoc corrections used in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Quasi-1D Downfolding of the GW Self-Energy for Accurate and Efficient Electronic Structure Prediction in Semiconductor Nanotubes
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of the dielectric function and quasiparticle band structure in semiconductor nanotubes is critical for designing high-brightness, low-droop nanoscale LEDs and lasers. However, applying the many-body GW approximation directly to these quasi-one-dimensional systems is computationally prohibitive due to the massive supercells required. This bottleneck hinders the rational design of novel nanotube-based optoelectronic materials with tailored band gaps and optical properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the GW self-energy of a parent bulk semiconductor, which captures its essential many-body screening, can be downfolded onto the Bloch state basis of a specific nanotube to yield an accurate, material-specific, non-local potential for the quasi-1D system, enabling efficient computation of its electronic properties.
proposals.method: We will first compute the full GW self-energy, Σ(ω), for the relevant bulk semiconductor (e.g., wurtzite GaN for nitride nanotubes) using established plane-wave codes. For a target nanotube with defined chirality, we will construct its quasi-1D Bloch states from the bulk band structure via zone-folding. The key step is to project the 3D Σ(ω) onto this reduced basis, effectively integrating out the irrelevant electronic degrees of freedom. This yields a downfolded, frequency-dependent self-energy operator for the nanotube, which will be used to solve the quasi-1D Dyson's equation and compute the dielectric function and quasiparticle bands.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computationally tractable, *ab initio* GW-level framework for predicting the electronic and optical properties of semiconductor nanotubes. It will directly enable the accurate design of nanotube geometries for specific emission wavelengths and reduced efficiency droop at high current densities, bridging a critical gap between many-body theory and nanoscale LED engineering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the conceptual framework of Hamiltonian downfolding from quantum chemistry to the GW approximation, creating a hybrid 3D-to-1D projection scheme that bypasses the need for a full supercell GW calculation, an approach not previously applied to nanotube optoelectronics.

proposals.title: Quantitative Nanoscale Dielectric Mapping of Carbon Nanotube Heterostructures via GW-Validated Scanning Capacitance Microscopy
proposals.motivation: The dielectric function is a fundamental property governing charge screening, exciton binding, and carrier transport in low-dimensional semiconductors like carbon nanotubes (CNTs). Pre-2026, while GW calculations predict complex dielectric landscapes near defects and heterojunctions, experimental validation has been limited to spatially averaged techniques. A method for direct, quantitative nanoscale dielectric mapping is critically needed to understand and engineer electronic edge states and correlation effects in CNT-based devices for high-brightness, droop-free light emission.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by using ab initio GW calculations of the dielectric function for specific CNT chiralities as a quantitative reference library, scanning capacitance microscopy (SCM) signals can be deconvoluted to produce accurate, spatially resolved dielectric maps, directly revealing electronic modifications at defects and interfaces not captured by standard theory.
proposals.method: We will first perform high-accuracy GW computations (using the BerkeleyGW package or similar) to generate reference dielectric functions for a series of pristine and defect-engineered single-wall CNT chiralities (e.g., (n,m) from (6,5) to (10,9)). These will be used to calibrate SCM measurements on corresponding chemically synthesized CNTs transferred to insulating substrates. A custom SCM probe with a conductive, shielded tip will acquire local dC/dV signals, which will be quantitatively inverted to local dielectric constant using a finite-element model informed by the GW references. This combined platform will map dielectric profiles across intentional defects (e.g., Stone-Wales) and end termini.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative, nanoscale dielectric mapping platform for 1D materials, moving SCM beyond qualitative imaging. It will provide direct experimental validation of many-body dielectric predictions and reveal new electronic states at topological defects, directly informing the design of CNT-based fin-LEDs and nanoscale lasers where dielectric engineering is key to suppressing non-radiative recombination and efficiency droop at high current densities.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in using GW theory not just for prediction, but as a quantitative calibration standard for scanning probe microscopy, transforming SCM into a validated nanometrology tool specifically for 1D systems, which has not been achieved pre-2026.

proposals.title: Quasiparticle Band Structures and Optical Response of III-Nitride Nanotubes via the GW Approximation and Bethe-Salpeter Equation
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of nitride-based LEDs, especially at high current densities, is limited by efficiency droop, which is influenced by complex many-body effects like carrier localization and Auger recombination. Accurate prediction of optical properties, such as the dielectric function, is crucial for designing novel nanostructures like nanotubes that could mitigate these losses. Standard density functional theory (DFT) with local or semi-local functionals systematically underestimates band gaps and fails to describe excitonic effects critical for optical response.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the GW approximation to compute quasiparticle band structures, followed by solving the Bethe-Salpeter equation (BSE) for optical spectra, will yield quantitatively accurate dielectric functions for III-nitride (e.g., GaN, InGaN) nanotubes. This will reveal if their reduced dimensionality and modified dielectric screening offer fundamentally superior radiative rates or suppressed Auger coefficients compared to planar quantum wells.
proposals.method: We will employ an ab initio workflow using plane-wave pseudopotential DFT (e.g., Quantum ESPRESSO) to obtain ground-state electronic structures for single-walled GaN and InGaN nanotubes of varying chirality and diameter. The GW self-energy corrections will be computed using the G0W0 approximation, as implemented in codes like BerkeleyGW or Yambo, to obtain accurate quasiparticle band gaps and dispersions. Subsequently, the frequency-dependent dielectric function ε(ω) will be calculated by solving the BSE for coupled electron-hole excitations, explicitly including excitonic effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first first-principles, many-body-derived optical constants for III-nitride nanotubes, establishing a reliable computational benchmark. It will directly inform the design of next-generation, droop-mitigating fin-LEDs and nanoscale lasers by identifying nanotube geometries with optimized absorption and emission profiles. The results will bridge high-accuracy electronic structure theory with the materials challenges in high-brightness, high-current optoelectronics.
proposals.novelty: While GW and BSE are standard for bulk and 2D materials, their systematic application to III-nitride nanotubes—a promising but less-explored architecture for LEDs—is novel. This approach moves beyond the effective-mass or simple DFT models typically used for these systems, directly probing the interplay of quantum confinement, dielectric screening, and many-body effects on their potential for high-performance devices.

proposals.title: Predictive First-Principles Dielectric Screening in High-Brightness Nitride LEDs: A Hybrid DFT-GW-BSE Framework for Droop Mitigation
proposals.motivation: The efficiency droop in high-current GaN-based LEDs remains a critical bottleneck for solid-state lighting and lasing, limiting maximum brightness. A key, underexplored factor is the role of dielectric screening in Auger recombination and carrier transport, which standard DFT severely misestimates. An accurate, predictive first-principles methodology for dielectric properties is therefore essential to understand and design droop-free, high-brightness emitters.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a sequential hybrid DFT-GW-BSE workflow will yield quantitatively accurate frequency-dependent dielectric tensors for nitride semiconductors, revealing that engineered dielectric screening at heterointerfaces and in fin-LED geometries can suppress Auger processes and mitigate efficiency droop at high current densities.
proposals.method: We will implement a robust, automated workflow using the VASP or Quantum ESPRESSO codes. Initial geometries of bulk GaN, InGaN alloys, and fin-LED facet models will be optimized using the HSE06 hybrid functional. Quasiparticle band structures will be computed using the G0W0 approximation atop the hybrid DFT ground state. The frequency-dependent dielectric function, including excitonic effects, will be calculated by solving the Bethe-Salpeter Equation (BSE) for the optical polarization function, using the GW-corrected eigenvalues and wavefunctions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first ab initio dielectric database for nitride LED materials under operational strain and doping conditions, directly linking microscopic screening to Auger coefficients. It will establish a general computational framework for predictive dielectric property calculation, moving beyond semi-empirical models. The insights will guide the design of facet-selective contacts and nanoscale geometries in fin-LEDs to optimize screening and reduce droop.
proposals.novelty: While GW-BSE is used for band gaps and absorption spectra, its systematic application to predict the full dielectric tensor—especially its off-diagonal components and spatial dependence in device-relevant nanostructures—for understanding efficiency droop is a novel integration of advanced computational materials science with core LED engineering challenges.

proposals.title: Substrate-Engineered Excitonic Screening in 2D Materials: A First-Principles BSE Study of Adsorbate-Modified MoS2 and Graphene
proposals.motivation: The performance of 2D-material-based LEDs and lasers is fundamentally governed by excitonic physics, which is highly sensitive to the dielectric environment. Pre-2026 models often treat substrates as static, uniform dielectric backgrounds, neglecting how surface reconstructions or intentional adsorbate patterning locally and asymmetrically modify electron-hole interactions. A quantitative, predictive framework linking atomic-scale surface modifications to excitonic properties is critical for designing next-generation, high-brightness, droop-free optoelectronic interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific adsorbate coverages or surface reconstructions on substrates like MoS2 and graphene will induce a spatially anisotropic, non-local modification of the dielectric screening, leading to predictable and tunable shifts in exciton binding energies and oscillator strengths, which can be captured by a substrate-integrated Bethe-Salpeter equation (BSE) approach.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) as implemented in Quantum ESPRESSO to compute the ground-state electronic structure of complex surfaces (e.g., S-vacancy reconstructions on MoS2, hydrogenated graphene). The screened Coulomb interaction (W) and electron-hole interaction kernel will be computed using the GW approximation and the BSE, respectively, via the BerkeleyGW package. The key innovation is constructing a supercell that explicitly includes the substrate surface modification, allowing the dielectric matrix ε^-1(r,r') to be computed from first principles for the combined system, rather than using a model dielectric function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first ab initio maps linking specific surface terminations to excitonic properties for designer substrates. It will deliver a computational framework to quantitatively predict how substrate engineering can be used as a knob to control optical transition strengths and exciton stability, directly informing the design of chip-scale LEDs and lasers with tailored emission characteristics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond the standard practice of treating the substrate as a passive dielectric constant; here, the atomically modified substrate surface is an explicit, integral part of the excitonic Hamiltonian, enabling the prediction of emergent optical selection rules and binding energies not accessible via continuum models.

proposals.title: Nanoscale Dielectric Screening and Plasmonic Response in Chirality-Defined Carbon Nanotube Bundles
proposals.motivation: In carbon nanotube (CNT) assemblies, inter-tube dielectric screening critically governs charge transport and exciton dynamics, directly impacting the performance of optoelectronic devices like LEDs. However, conventional ensemble measurements obscure the role of specific chiralities and inter-tube geometries due to averaging over disorder. A precise, nanoscale understanding of screening in structurally defined few-tube systems is needed to inform the design of high-brightness, low-droop nanoscale light emitters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local dielectric function and plasmonic response in few-CNT assemblies are dictated by a quantifiable, geometry-dependent screening interaction, which can be directly mapped and correlated with the specific electronic band structures of the constituent tubes.
proposals.method: We will fabricate model systems using templated chemical vapor deposition and directed assembly to create well-defined few-tube bundles, including double-wall nanotubes and small ropes with pre-selected chiralities (e.g., (n,m) indices). These structures will be characterized via scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (s-SNOM) and nano-FTIR spectroscopy. We will map the local dielectric function and plasmonic resonances with ~20 nm spatial resolution, correlating optical maps with structural data from atomic force microscopy and Raman spectroscopy to isolate the screening contribution from specific inter-tube geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, nanoscale correlation between defined CNT chirality pairs, their geometric arrangement, and the resulting dielectric screening. It will yield quantitative design rules for inter-tube electronic coupling, directly informing strategies to mitigate non-radiative losses (droop) in nanoscale semiconductor assemblies for high-current, high-brightness applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving from disordered ensembles to precisely engineered few-tube 'molecules' and applying nano-optical mapping to directly probe screening physics at the individual interaction level, bypassing the averaging inherent in all previous optical studies of CNT bundles.
