proposals.title: Coupled-Oscillator Model for Dielectric Screening and Carrier Dynamics in Carbon Nanotube Ropes: Implications for High-Brightness Nanoscale LEDs
proposals.motivation: The performance of nanoscale light-emitting devices, such as the fin LEDs in your recent work, is critically limited by efficiency droop at high current densities, often linked to non-radiative Auger recombination and carrier leakage. In nanostructures like carbon nanotube (CNT) ropes, which are promising for nanophotonics, inter-tube dielectric screening fundamentally modifies carrier-carrier interactions and polarization response, but this collective effect is poorly understood. Quantifying this screening is essential to engineer materials where suppressed Auger recombination or enhanced radiative efficiency could enable droop-free, high-brightness emission.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inter-tube dielectric screening in CNT ropes can be mapped onto a coupled-oscillator model from molecular excitonics, where the collective dielectric response and resultant modulation of carrier interactions (e.g., Coulomb enhancement factors) are dictated by rope geometry (tube count, spacing, chirality) and can be tuned to suppress loss mechanisms dominant at high current.
proposals.method: We will model a CNT rope as an array of coupled polarizable cylinders, with each tube's optical response described by a frequency-dependent polarizability tensor derived from its band structure. Using a coupled dipole approximation (CDA) framework, we will compute the eigenmodes of the coupled system to obtain the rope's effective dielectric function and local field factors. This will be integrated with a rate equation model for carrier dynamics, parameterizing Auger and radiative rates based on the screened Coulomb interaction. The model will be validated against pre-2026 experimental data on isolated vs. bundled nanotube photoluminescence quenching and theoretical studies of CNT dielectric properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, scalable theoretical link between nanorope geometry and its effective dielectric and optoelectronic properties, directly quantifying screening's role in high-current performance metrics. It will deliver design principles for nanostructured materials where tailored dielectric environments can mitigate efficiency droop, contributing directly to the development of high-brightness, droop-free nanoscale emitters.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the well-established molecular exciton/coupled oscillator formalism—typically for molecular aggregates—to the mesoscale problem of dielectric screening in quasi-1D van der Waals solids, creating a unified framework to predict how collective polarization emerges from individual tube properties and geometry.

proposals.title: Droop-Free, High-Brightness Emission from Ultra-Short Carbon Nanotube Quantum Dots via Facet-Selective Contact Engineering
proposals.motivation: Achieving high-brightness light emission at high current densities without efficiency droop remains a critical challenge for LEDs. Recent work on fin LEDs and facet-selective contacts has shown a path to droop-free operation in III-V materials by controlling carrier injection and thermal transport. Extending these principles to ultra-short, quantum-confined carbon nanotube (CNT) segments could unlock new nanoscale, high-speed light sources with tunable emission.
proposals.hypothesis: Engineering asymmetric, facet-selective electrical contacts to ultra-short (<10 nm) single-chirality carbon nanotube segments will suppress Auger recombination and Joule heating, enabling droop-free, high-current electroluminescence by exploiting finite-size quantum confinement effects.
proposals.method: We will synthesize ultra-short, single-crystal CNT segments using solution-phase cutting and sorting techniques to achieve monodisperse lengths. Electron-beam lithography will be used to fabricate asymmetric metal contacts (e.g., Pd and Sc) to the ends of individual nanotubes, creating a facet-selective injection scheme. Electrical and optical characterization (I-V, electroluminescence intensity vs. current, time-resolved PL) will be performed. Results will be analyzed using a modified rate equation model incorporating finite-size quantization of electronic bands and non-radiative Auger coefficients derived from first-principles DFT calculations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials-agnostic design principle for droop-free nanoscale emitters by combining quantum confinement with engineered contacts. It will provide the first experimental demonstration of high-current, high-brightness electroluminescence from a carbon nanotube quantum dot, pushing the limits of miniaturization for on-chip light sources.
proposals.novelty: While facet-selective contacts are known for fin LEDs, their application to exploit the finite-length electronic structure of ultra-short nanotubes for droop suppression is unexplored. This merges quantum dot physics with advanced contact engineering in a 1D system.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Carbon Nanotube Chirality for Target Dielectric Response in High-Brightness, Droop-Free Optoelectronics
proposals.motivation: The performance of next-generation LEDs and nanoscale lasers is fundamentally limited by the electronic and optical properties of the active materials, particularly their dielectric function. While carbon nanotubes (CNTs) offer exceptional and tunable optoelectronic properties, the forward problem of predicting properties from a given chirality is well-studied. The inverse problem—designing the nanotube structure to achieve a specific, desired dielectric response—remains a critical, unsolved challenge for the rational engineering of high-efficiency, droop-free light emitters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained on first-principles calculations can accurately and efficiently map a target complex dielectric function to the specific chiral indices (n,m) of a single-walled carbon nanotube, enabling the inverse design of nanotube-based active regions for optoelectronics.
proposals.method: We will first generate a comprehensive dataset of complex dielectric functions for a wide range of (n,m) chiralities using density functional theory (DFT) and tight-binding calculations, validated against pre-2026 experimental data. This dataset will train a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) architecture, where the encoder learns a latent representation of the dielectric function and the decoder, conditioned on this representation, predicts the probability distribution over chiral indices. Bayesian optimization will then be used to query the model for nanotube structures that minimize the difference between their predicted dielectric function and a user-specified target.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic computational framework for the inverse design of carbon nanotubes based on optical response. It will directly contribute to the development of novel, high-brightness light-emitting materials by identifying optimal nanotube chiralities for reduced efficiency droop and enhanced light-matter interaction in nanoscale fin-LED and laser architectures.
proposals.novelty: While forward property prediction for CNTs is standard, inverting the structure-property relationship for the dielectric function—a continuous, complex-valued target—is a novel and non-trivial machine learning task. This approach moves beyond traditional trial-and-error or forward-screening methods to enable true target-driven design.

proposals.title: Ab Initio Calculation of Diamagnetic and Van Vleck Susceptibility in Semiconducting Nanotubes for Spintronic Applications
proposals.motivation: The development of efficient, high-brightness LEDs has been limited by efficiency droop at high current densities, a problem addressed in recent fin-LED designs by controlling carrier transport and recombination. Concurrently, the spin and magnetic properties of low-dimensional semiconductors are critical for emerging spintronic and quantum technologies, yet the magnetic susceptibility of specific nanostructures like semiconducting nanotubes remains poorly quantified. Understanding this fundamental property could reveal new pathways for magneto-optical device integration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the unique, curvature-induced band structure and quantum confinement in semiconducting single-wall carbon nanotubes (s-SWCNTs) and analogous III-V nanotubes will produce a measurable and tunable magnetic susceptibility, with significant contributions from both diamagnetic (orbital) and Van Vleck (second-order paramagnetic) terms.
proposals.method: We will employ first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations, as implemented in the SIESTA or Quantum ESPRESSO codes, to compute the electronic structure of chiral and zigzag s-SWCNTs and selected III-V nanotubes (e.g., BN, GaN). The magnetic susceptibility tensor will be calculated using linear response theory within the gauge-including projector-augmented wave (GIPAW) method, which accurately handles orbital currents. This will be complemented by tight-binding models, parameterized from DFT, to analyze trends across diameters and chiralities and to separate the orbital and Van Vleck contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first ab initio database of magnetic susceptibilities for a range of semiconducting nanotubes, directly linking their chiral structure to magnetic response. It will establish design principles for nanotubes in magneto-optical and spin-filtering devices, potentially identifying materials with enhanced susceptibility for sensor applications. The methodology will extend the analytical tools used for LED band engineering to a new fundamental property.
proposals.novelty: While DFT is routinely used for electronic band structure, its application to calculate the full magnetic susceptibility tensor for specific nanotube chiralities is not standard. The novelty lies in applying the high-precision, materials-specific computational techniques central to modern optoelectronics research to systematically map a previously underexplored physical property in these nanostructures.

proposals.title: Wrinkle-Engineered Band Structure and Radiative Efficiency in Monolayer Transition Metal Dichalcogenide Light-Emitting Diodes
proposals.motivation: The performance of 2D material-based optoelectronics, particularly LEDs, is critically limited by non-radiative recombination at high current densities, analogous to 'efficiency droop' in III-Nitride LEDs. While strain engineering via wrinkles is known to modify the electronic structure of 2D materials, its systematic application to control carrier dynamics and mitigate efficiency loss in operational 2D LEDs remains unexplored. This work bridges the gap between fundamental strain physics and the applied challenge of achieving high-brightness, droop-free emission from atomically thin semiconductors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled, periodic wrinkle networks in monolayer WS2 and MoS2 can create spatially varying strain fields that locally modulate the band gap and carrier effective masses, thereby suppressing Auger recombination and enhancing radiative efficiency at high injection currents.
proposals.method: We will fabricate wrinkle networks by transferring CVD-grown monolayers onto pre-patterned elastomeric substrates (PDMS) with defined pillar arrays. The strain distribution will be characterized via Raman and photoluminescence mapping. These strained monolayers will be integrated into vertical LED structures using facet-selective contact schemes, inspired by recent fin-LED architectures, to ensure efficient carrier injection. Electronic structure modifications will be calculated using density functional theory (DFT) with the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP), and the impact on radiative and Auger recombination rates will be modeled via a drift-diffusion framework coupled with a k·p perturbation method.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative link between engineered wrinkle morphology and the high-current electro-optical performance of 2D TMD LEDs. It will provide design principles for strain-engineering 2D materials to overcome efficiency droop, a major bottleneck. The findings will advance the development of high-brightness, atomically thin light sources for on-chip photonics.
proposals.novelty: While wrinkles are often studied as defects, this proposal treats them as a deliberate design element to solve the high-current efficiency problem in 2D LEDs, a novel application of strain engineering. The combination of wrinkle patterning with advanced, droop-mitigating contact architectures from III-Nitride research is a unique cross-material methodology.

proposals.title: Droop-Free, High-Brightness Nanotube LEDs via Chemically Doped Axial p-n Junctions
proposals.motivation: Achieving high brightness and efficiency in nanoscale light-emitting diodes (LEDs) at high current densities is limited by efficiency droop, often exacerbated by non-uniform carrier injection in electrostatically gated structures. Chemically stable, axial p-n junctions in one-dimensional semiconductors could provide superior electrostatic control and carrier confinement compared to purely electrostatic or heterostructure-based devices, directly addressing the high-current performance challenges central to recent fin-LED architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that creating an axial p-n junction within a single carbon nanotube or semiconductor nanowire via segment-selective chemical doping will enable droop-free, high-brightness electroluminescence by ensuring balanced, high-density electron and hole injection directly into the same ultrasmall active region.
proposals.method: We will synthesize long, single-crystal semiconductor nanotubes (e.g., carbon nanotubes or analogous inorganic nanotubes) and use a two-step, spatially masked chemical doping process. First, a segment will be exposed to an oxidizing agent (e.g., F4TCNQ) for p-type doping, then a protected adjacent segment will be exposed to a reducing agent (e.g., polyethyleneimine) for n-type doping, creating an abrupt axial junction. These doped nanotube segments will be integrated into a fin-LED device architecture using facet-selective metallic contacts (Ni for p-segment, Yb/Al for n-segment) to form the diode. Device performance will be characterized by measuring current-voltage characteristics, electroluminescence intensity, spectrum, and external quantum efficiency as a function of current density.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel nanoscale LED where the junction is defined by chemical composition rather than external fields, leading to a more robust and scalable device for integrated photonics. It will provide fundamental insight into carrier injection and recombination physics in chemically doped 1D junctions at extreme current densities, directly contributing to the development of droop-free, high-brightness light sources for chip-scale applications.
proposals.novelty: While electrostatic gating is common for creating nanotube p-n junctions, using localized, stable chemical doping to form an intrinsic axial junction for high-current LED operation is a significant and unexplored materials integration challenge. This approach merges the chemical robustness of doped semiconductors with the electro-optical advantages of the fin-LED geometry.

proposals.title: Droop-Free, High-Brightness Visible Light Emission from Chiral-Index-Selected Single-Walled Carbon Nanotubes via Facet-Selective Electrical Injection
proposals.motivation: Achieving high-brightness, efficient light emission in the visible spectrum with one-dimensional (1D) semiconductors remains a significant challenge. Conventional III-nitride LEDs suffer from efficiency droop at high current densities, limiting brightness. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) offer a unique 1D platform with bandgaps tunable by chirality, but electroluminescence (EL) has been largely limited to the near-infrared, with visible emission being weak and inefficient.
proposals.hypothesis: By combining chirality-selected single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) with a facet-selective contact architecture—analogous to the successful fin-LED design—we can achieve efficient, droop-free electrical injection and high-brightness visible light emission across specific wavelengths (e.g., red, green).
proposals.method: We will synthesize and isolate specific (n,m) SWCNT chiralities (e.g., (6,4), (7,5)) known from photoluminescence studies to emit in the visible range. These nanotubes will be integrated into a fin-like device geometry using advanced nanofabrication. A key innovation is the application of facet-selective electrical contacts, where different metal work functions are deposited on specific facets of the nanotube bundle or aligned array to create a lateral p-i-n junction, enabling uniform carrier injection along the 1D channel. This will be coupled with optical microcavities to enhance light extraction and spectral purity.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the first high-brightness, electrically driven visible light source based on carbon nanotubes, directly addressing the efficiency droop problem. It will establish a novel materials and device architecture paradigm for 1D semiconductor optoelectronics, potentially enabling ultra-narrowband emitters for displays and on-chip photonics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of chirality-specific 1D materials engineering with a facet-selective contact strategy—a method proven for droop suppression in III-V fin-LEDs but never applied to carbon nanotubes for visible electroluminescence. This moves beyond simple nanotube film devices to a designed, high-current injection architecture.

proposals.title: Electrically Pumped Lasing from Droop-Free Nanotube p-n Junctions via Facet-Selective Optical Feedback
proposals.motivation: Electrically driven nanoscale lasers are critical for integrated photonic circuits and on-chip optical interconnects. While droop-free fin LEDs have achieved record high brightness and submicrometer lasing under optical pumping, a compact, electrically injected laser remains a key challenge. Demonstrating lasing from a directly biased nanostructure would bridge a major gap between efficient light-emitting devices and practical laser sources for chip-scale applications.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a high-quality optical cavity, such as a photonic crystal or distributed Bragg reflector, with an electrically pumped, droop-free nanotube p-n junction, we can achieve population inversion and transition from spontaneous to stimulated emission, resulting in electrically driven lasing.
proposals.method: We will fabricate p-n junction nanowires/nanotubes using selective-area epitaxy, incorporating facet-selective contacts to enable high-current injection without efficiency droop. A one-dimensional photonic crystal cavity will be defined around the active region via electron-beam lithography and dry etching to provide optical feedback. Device performance will be characterized by measuring light-current-voltage curves, spectral linewidth narrowing, and far-field patterns to identify the lasing threshold and coherence properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the first electrically pumped laser based on the droop-free fin LED architecture, establishing a pathway to ultra-efficient nanoscale coherent light sources. It will provide fundamental insights into the carrier dynamics and photon management required to overcome loss and achieve lasing under high electrical injection.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines the high-current, droop-free capability of facet-contacted nanostructures with a tailored nanophotonic cavity, moving beyond mere high brightness to achieve coherent emission from an electrically injected device, which has not been realized in this material system.

proposals.title: Droop-Free, High-Brightness Emission from Carbon Nanotubes via Purcell Enhancement in Deterministic Fin Microcavities
proposals.motivation: Achieving high-brightness, droop-free light emission at high current densities is a critical challenge for next-generation LEDs and nanoscale lasers. While recent work on III-nitride fin LEDs has shown a path to suppressing efficiency droop, extending these principles to low-dimensional, solution-processable emitters like single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) could enable novel integrated photonic sources. SWCNTs offer unique spectral purity and stability but suffer from low radiative rates and limited light extraction, constraining their practical brightness.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a semiconducting SWCNT into a high-quality-factor (Q) fin microcavity with facet-selective electrical contacts, we hypothesize that the Purcell effect will dramatically enhance the radiative rate, enabling droop-free, high-current operation and a significant increase in external quantum efficiency and output brightness.
proposals.method: We will fabricate suspended, air-bridged fin microcavities from silicon nitride or gallium nitride using electron-beam lithography and reactive ion etching, designed to resonate at the E11 excitonic emission wavelength of chirality-selected (6,5) SWCNTs. Nanotubes will be deposited via dielectrophoretic assembly for deterministic placement across the cavity mode maximum. Facet-selective, transparent contacts analogous to the fin-LED architecture will be patterned for efficient electrical injection. We will characterize the Purcell factor via time-resolved photoluminescence and measure light-current (L-I) characteristics to quantify droop suppression and brightness enhancement compared to unstructured devices.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel device platform combining the electronic merits of carbon nanotubes with the photonic control of fin microcavities, directly addressing the brightness bottleneck for nanotube LEDs. It will provide the first demonstration of Purcell-enhanced, electrically driven emission from a nanotube in a deterministic cavity, contributing fundamental insights into high-current exciton dynamics in nanoscale systems.
proposals.novelty: While Purcell enhancement in passive nanotube-cavity systems is known, the novelty lies in the active, electrically driven fin-cavity design adapted from droop-free LED research, enabling the direct study and mitigation of efficiency droop in a fundamentally one-dimensional emitter under high injection.

proposals.title: Droop-Free On-Chip Light Sources: Efficient Coupling of Fin LED Emission into Silicon Photonic Waveguides
proposals.motivation: Silicon photonics is the dominant platform for on-chip optical interconnects and sensing, but it lacks efficient, scalable, and electrically driven light sources due to silicon's indirect bandgap. Integrating high-performance III-V emitters remains challenging due to material incompatibility and coupling losses. A compact, droop-free light emitter that can be monolithically integrated and efficiently coupled to silicon waveguides is a critical missing component for advancing integrated photonic circuits.
proposals.hypothesis: The facet-selective contact architecture of droop-free fin LEDs, which enables high-brightness lasing, can be adapted to a geometry that maximizes the evanescent coupling efficiency of its emitted light into an adjacent silicon nitride or silicon-on-insulator waveguide, creating a scalable on-chip light source with low insertion loss.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate InGaN/GaN fin LEDs using selective-area growth on silicon, leveraging pre-2026 facet engineering techniques to control emission directionality. The fins will be positioned and shaped (e.g., tapered) using electron-beam lithography to optimize the overlap between the LED optical mode and the fundamental TE mode of a pre-patterned silicon nitride waveguide. Coupling efficiency will be simulated using 3D finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) methods. Experimental characterization will involve measuring the waveguide-coupled output power versus injection current and comparing it to the free-space emission to quantify the coupling efficiency and any remaining droop.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel, monolithically integrable light source with direct, efficient coupling to silicon photonic waveguides, addressing a key bottleneck in the field. It will provide a quantitative framework for designing high-efficiency emitter-waveguide interfaces using fin geometries. The results could enable new architectures for on-chip optical communication and lab-on-a-chip sensors.
proposals.novelty: While integrating LEDs with waveguides has been studied, the use of droop-free fin LEDs with facet-selective contacts for this purpose is unexplored. The novelty lies in co-designing the fin's electronic structure (for droop-free operation) and its photonic geometry (for optimal evanescent coupling) specifically for silicon photonic integration.

proposals.title: Droop-Free, High-Brightness Single-Photon Emission from Localized Defect States in Semiconductor Nanotubes
proposals.motivation: Efficient, high-brightness single-photon sources are critical for quantum communication and computing, but scaling quantum systems often faces efficiency droop at high currents. Pre-2026 work on droop-free fin LEDs demonstrates a path to high current density operation without efficiency loss, a principle that could revolutionize quantum light sources if applied to defect-localized emitters.
proposals.hypothesis: By engineering isolated, atomically precise defects within the band structure of semiconductor nanotubes and applying facet-selective contact architectures, we can create a single-photon source that operates without efficiency droop at high excitation currents, achieving unprecedented brightness and on-demand emission purity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize single-crystal methylammonium lead iodide perovskite and III-nitride nanotubes via controlled vapor-phase growth, introducing specific defect complexes during synthesis. Using facet-selective electron and hole contacts, akin to the fin-LED architecture, we will electrically pump individual defect sites. Single-photon purity (g2(0)) and brightness will be characterized via Hanbury Brown-Twiss interferometry under high current density, while density functional theory (DFT) simulations will model the defect electronic structure and carrier localization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for electrically driven, high-brightness quantum emitters by directly translating macroscopic droop-free LED concepts to the quantum defect scale. It will provide a materials and device blueprint for integrating deterministic single-photon sources into scalable photonic circuits.
proposals.novelty: The integration of macroscopic, droop-free contact engineering with atomic-scale defect physics in a nanotube geometry is a novel synthesis of top-down device physics and bottom-up quantum materials design, moving beyond passive defect studies to actively engineered quantum light-emitting diodes.

proposals.title: Substrate-Free, Droop-Free Electroluminescence from Suspended III-Nitride Nanotube p-n Junctions
proposals.motivation: In high-brightness, high-current LEDs, efficiency droop remains a critical limitation, often exacerbated by dielectric effects from the substrate, such as strain, thermal impedance, and carrier scattering. Recent advances in fin-LED architectures have shown droop-free operation by controlling current injection, but the intrinsic emission properties of the active material remain obscured by substrate interactions. A platform that isolates the p-n junction from all external dielectric environments is needed to probe the fundamental limits of radiative recombination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fabricating a p-n junction within a fully suspended semiconductor nanotube will eliminate efficiency-droop mechanisms related to the substrate, revealing intrinsic, droop-free electroluminescence up to extreme current densities by maximizing light extraction and minimizing non-radiative pathways.
proposals.method: We will fabricate suspended nanotube LEDs using selective area epitaxy of III-nitride materials (e.g., InGaN/GaN) on patterned nanorod templates, followed by a controlled wet-etch to remove the underlying substrate, creating free-standing tubes. Facet-selective contacts, analogous to those used in fin-LEDs, will be deposited via angled evaporation to establish p- and n-type electrical injection at opposite ends of the nanotube. The emission properties will be characterized via micro-electroluminescence and time-resolved photoluminescence under high current injection, comparing suspended devices to substrate-bound controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first direct measurement of intrinsic electroluminescence efficiency limits in a substrate-free semiconductor p-n junction. It will quantify the specific contribution of dielectric substrate effects to efficiency droop and provide a novel, high-brightness emitter architecture with superior thermal management and light extraction for potential laser applications.
proposals.novelty: While suspended nanowires have been studied for photodetectors and lasers, the implementation of a fully operational, electrically injected p-n junction inside a *suspended nanotube*—a truly substrate-free LED—is unprecedented. This merges the concepts of droop-free fin-LED contacts with a complete removal of the dielectric environment, a step beyond current state-of-the-art.

proposals.title: Droop-Free Light Emission from Semiconductor Nanowires via Electrostatic p-n Junctions
proposals.motivation: Efficiency droop at high current densities remains a critical limitation for high-brightness LEDs, including those based on nanowires. Recent work on fin LEDs has demonstrated a novel solution using electrostatic, rather than chemical, p-n junctions to achieve droop-free operation. Extending this electrostatic junction principle to semiconductor nanowires could unlock high-brightness, droop-free light sources in a versatile, bottom-up material platform.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the facet-selective contact and electrostatic p-n junction concept to semiconductor nanowires will enable high-current, droop-free electroluminescence by spatially separating electron and hole injection to suppress Auger recombination.
proposals.method: We will fabricate vertical nanowire arrays using selective-area epitaxy of III-nitride (e.g., InGaN/GaN) and III-arsenide (e.g., InGaAs) materials. Using electron-beam lithography and angled deposition, we will pattern facet-selective metal contacts to create separate electron- and hole-injecting electrodes on distinct nanowire sidewalls. Device performance will be characterized by measuring light output versus current (L-I) curves, efficiency droop, and spectral stability. The electrostatic potential profile and carrier distribution will be modeled using finite-element Poisson and drift-diffusion solvers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for high-brightness nanowire LEDs, directly addressing efficiency droop. It will provide a materials-agnostic blueprint for achieving high-current operation in 1D nanostructures, potentially benefiting applications in micro-LED displays, optical interconnects, and single-photon sources.
proposals.novelty: While electrostatic junctions have been explored in 2D fin LEDs, their application to achieve droop-free operation in bottom-up semiconductor nanowires is unexplored. The approach decouples the challenging problem of doping from the requirement for efficient carrier injection, a significant departure from conventional nanowire LED design.

proposals.title: Atomic-Scale Origins of Defect Initiation: Linking Single Nanoscale Mounds to Dislocation Nucleation in III-Nitride LEDs
proposals.motivation: In high-brightness, high-current LEDs, efficiency droop and reliability are critically limited by defect generation. While macroscopic defect densities are well-studied, the precise atomic-scale event where a single nanoscale surface irregularity triggers the first dislocation remains unknown. Understanding this initial nucleation is essential for designing droop-free, ultra-reliable optoelectronic devices, as highlighted by recent work on facet-selective contacts and fin LEDs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a critical, atomically-defined local strain field, generated by a specific atomic configuration of a single epitaxial mound, directly determines the nucleation site and type of the first threading dislocation in III-nitride heterostructures.
proposals.method: We will combine molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using Stillinger-Weber and Tersoff-like potentials for GaN to model mound evolution under strain, with density functional theory (DFT) calculations on extracted cluster models to identify electronic structure signatures at the nucleation core. Atomic-scale validation will be pursued via in-situ scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) of metalorganic chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD) growth on patterned substrates, correlating mound geometry from atomic force microscopy (AFM) with subsequent dislocation formation observed via weak-beam dark-field TEM.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between a specific atomic surface feature and the birth of a critical extended defect, moving beyond statistical descriptions. It will provide design rules for growth conditions and surface treatments to suppress defect nucleation at its origin, directly impacting the development of next-generation high-power, droop-free LEDs and lasers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in isolating and studying the *single* defect-initiating event, rather than ensembles, and directly connecting atomic-scale topography from simulations to experimental observation of the very first dislocation, a scale previously inaccessible.

proposals.title: Stochastic Carrier Dynamics in Nanoscale Perovskite and Fin-LEDs: Modeling Efficiency Droop and Spectral Stability Under High Current Densities
proposals.motivation: The drive for ultra-high brightness in submicrometer LEDs, as demonstrated in recent droop-free fin architectures, pushes current densities to extremes where local carrier densities and temperatures fluctuate significantly. In nanoscale perovskite films and semiconductor fins, thermal energy (k_B T) becomes comparable to confinement energies and local potential variations, making deterministic drift-diffusion models insufficient. Ignoring these stochastic effects limits our understanding of efficiency droop origins, spectral shift, and long-term degradation at the chip scale.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating stochastic thermal fluctuations into carrier transport and recombination models will reveal new, dominant non-radiative loss pathways at high current densities and explain the superior droop resistance observed in facet-engineered fin LEDs compared to planar nanoscale perovskite structures.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-scale kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) framework coupled with a stochastic drift-diffusion-Poisson solver. The model will explicitly treat carrier injection, transport, and radiative/Auger recombination as stochastic processes, with local band edges and trap energies modulated by thermal lattice fluctuations. Parameters will be derived from first-principles DFT calculations for methylammonium lead iodide perovskite and III-N fin-LED materials. Simulation will focus on comparing the temporal and spatial correlation of carrier density fluctuations in a standard nanorod versus a facet-contacted fin geometry under high injection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles-based simulation tool for predicting the high-current performance limits of nanoscale emitters. It will deliver a mechanistic explanation for efficiency droop rooted in fluctuation-induced Auger recombination and identify optimal nanoscale geometries (like fins) that suppress these stochastic losses. This bridges the materials-focused study of perovskite decomposition with the device-level success of fin LEDs.
proposals.novelty: While stochastic methods exist for molecular dynamics, their application to high-current carrier dynamics in state-of-the-art nanoscale LEDs is novel. The direct comparison between perovskite and III-N fin structures under a unified fluctuation-based model will uncover universal versus material-specific scaling laws for brightness and droop.

proposals.title: First-Principles Determination of Defect Energetics in Droop-Free Fin-LED Materials: Beyond Empirical Potentials for High-Brightness Operation
proposals.motivation: Achieving high-brightness, droop-free LEDs, as demonstrated in recent fin-LED architectures, is critically limited by defect-mediated non-radiative recombination at high current densities. Classical empirical potentials, while computationally efficient, often lack the accuracy to reliably predict defect formation and transition energies in complex semiconductor heterostructures and novel materials like halide perovskites, leading to unreliable predictions of efficiency limits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations, employing hybrid functionals and many-body perturbation theory corrections, will reveal significantly different defect energy landscapes compared to empirical potential models, identifying previously overlooked point defects that control efficiency droop in high-current fin-LEDs.
proposals.method: We will perform high-throughput DFT calculations using the VASP code with the HSE06 hybrid functional to accurately model electronic band structures. Defect formation energies and charge transition levels will be computed for candidate fin-LED materials (e.g., InGaN/GaN, perovskite systems) using the standard supercell approach and finite-size corrections. For critical defects, we will apply GW and Bethe-Salpeter equation (BSE) methods to obtain quasiparticle energies and optical transition strengths, benchmarking against experimental luminescence and droop characteristics from fabricated devices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitatively reliable map of defect energetics in state-of-the-art fin-LED materials, directly linking specific atomic-scale defects to macroscopic efficiency droop. It will establish a first-principles framework for screening materials and doping schemes to suppress defect formation, guiding the experimental design of next-generation, high-brightness light sources.
proposals.novelty: While DFT is standard for bulk materials, its systematic application to defect physics in the unique, facet-engineered geometries of droop-free fin-LEDs—moving beyond the empirical potentials common in device-scale modeling—represents a novel integration of accurate quantum mechanics with targeted device performance challenges.

proposals.title: Interacting Carbon Defect Complexes in III-Nitrides: A First-Principles Study of Non-Radiative Recombination at High Current Densities
proposals.motivation: In GaN-based LEDs, efficiency droop at high current densities remains a critical limitation for solid-state lighting and lasing. While point defects like carbon are known non-radiative recombination centers, their behavior is typically modeled in isolation. At the high defect concentrations present in real devices, especially in novel high-current structures like fin LEDs, defect-defect interactions likely dominate carrier dynamics, but this regime is poorly understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that at high concentrations relevant to device operation, carbon defects in GaN form interacting complexes whose electronic structure and non-radiative capture cross-sections differ fundamentally from those of isolated defects, explaining anomalous efficiency loss under high injection.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) with hybrid functionals (HSE06) to model supercells of wurtzite GaN containing high concentrations of carbon substitutions (C_N) and complexes (e.g., C_N-C_N pairs, C_N-V_N). We will calculate formation energies, charge transition levels, and electronic wavefunctions. Non-radiative capture coefficients will be estimated using the static coupling theory, explicitly evaluating the electron-phonon coupling matrix elements for defect complexes to compare with isolated defects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first ab initio map of how defect interactions modify non-radiative pathways in III-nitrides at high concentrations. It will deliver quantitative parameters for advanced device simulations, directly informing the design of droop-mitigation strategies in high-brightness LEDs and lasers by targeting the stability of detrimental defect complexes.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond the standard single-defect model to systematically study interacting defect ensembles addresses a known but neglected complexity in semiconductor physics, directly linking atomic-scale interactions to the macroscopic droop phenomenon in state-of-the-art LED architectures.

proposals.title: Droop-Free Fin LED Design via Transferable Tight-Binding Models for High-Current Electronic Structure
proposals.motivation: The development of high-brightness, droop-free LEDs, such as the fin LEDs demonstrated in recent work, requires understanding complex electronic states under high injection. First-principles density functional theory (DFT) is computationally prohibitive for simulating the large, complex device geometries and non-equilibrium carrier densities relevant to these systems. A faster, yet accurate, electronic structure method is needed to model carrier dynamics and band engineering at the scale of operational devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that semi-empirical tight-binding (TB) models, parameterized from high-fidelity DFT calculations, can be transferred across related III-nitride and perovskite material systems to enable rapid, predictive simulations of electronic structure and optical properties in high-current LED architectures.
proposals.method: We will develop a Slater-Koster parameter database for InGaN alloys and methylammonium lead iodide perovskites by fitting TB band structures to DFT-calculated band edges and effective masses. The transferability will be tested by applying these parameters to heterostructures and superlattices not included in the fitting set. We will implement these models in a real-space TB solver coupled with a non-equilibrium Green's function (NEGF) formalism to compute current injection and recombination profiles in fin LED geometries, explicitly modeling facet-selective contacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computational toolkit for the rapid, atomistic design of droop-free LED architectures, directly linking atomic-scale material choices to device-scale performance metrics like efficiency droop and lasing thresholds. It will establish a protocol for generating transferable TB parameters for emerging optoelectronic materials.
proposals.novelty: While TB is standard in semiconductor physics, its application as a transferable, high-throughput tool for the combined design of novel materials (like perovskites) and complex nanoscale device geometries (like fin LEDs) is novel. This bridges a critical gap between ab initio accuracy and device-scale simulation.

ideas.title: Efficient GW Approximation for Nanotubes Using a 1D-Specific Formalism and Truncated Coulomb Interaction
ideas.description: This research develops a GW computational approach specifically designed for the quasi-one-dimensional electronic structure of carbon nanotubes. It implements a tailored basis set and a truncated Coulomb interaction to correctly handle the anisotropic screening and eliminate artificial interactions from periodic boundary conditions. This enables the efficient and accurate first-principles calculation of dielectric functions and quasiparticle band gaps for nanotubes of varying chirality and diameter. The method provides a robust platform for predicting optical properties and screening effects in nanotube-based electronic and optoelectronic devices.

ideas.title: Apply GW Approximation from Bulk Semiconductors to Nanotubes via a Quasi-1D Downfolding Approach
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry—where the electronic Hamiltonian is downfolded into an active orbital space to manage complexity—to the problem of applying the GW approximation to nanotubes. The analogous solution involves focusing computational resources on the most relevant electronic degrees of freedom. The adapted research idea is to compute the GW self-energy for the bulk semiconductor from which the nanotube is conceptually derived (e.g., graphite for carbon nanotubes). This 3D self-energy is then downfolded or projected onto the quasi-one-dimensional Bloch state basis of the specific nanotube. This provides a material-specific, non-local potential that captures key many-body screening effects, enabling the efficient calculation of accurate dielectric functions and quasiparticle band structures for nanotubes without performing a full GW calculation on the massive nanotube supercell.

ideas.title: GW-verified tips for atomically precise quantum transport in nanotubes
ideas.description: This research direction combines tip characterization for atomic manipulation with GW-computed dielectric functions for nanotubes to achieve atomically precise quantum device fabrication. The GW method provides an accurate, material-specific description of the nanotube's dielectric response to the electric field from a verified scanning probe tip. This allows for the ab initio design of voltage pulses that leverage this screened interaction to manipulate atoms or defects on the nanotube surface with unprecedented reliability. The resulting capability is a closed-loop platform where quantum transport properties of the constructed atomic-scale device can be measured and directly compared to predictions from the same GW-based framework used to build it.

ideas.title: Probe Substrate-Induced Dielectric Screening in Metallic Nanotubes on Complex Surfaces
ideas.description: This research direction applies a unified tight-binding and correlation analysis framework to investigate metallic carbon nanotubes deposited on substrates with complex reconstructions or adsorbate layers, such as reconstructed silicon surfaces or graphene on transition metal dichalcogenides. The core synergy is using the metallic nanotube as a sensitive, one-dimensional probe of substrate-induced dielectric screening and charge redistribution, while simultaneously measuring how the substrate's inhomogeneous potential modifies the nanotube's own dielectric function and low-energy plasmon modes. This enables a coupled analysis where changes in the nanotube's electronic response directly report on the local surface electronic properties of the complex substrate, offering a novel method to map substrate effects with nanoscale resolution along the tube axis.

ideas.title: Modeling dynamic tip-chiral nanotube dielectric interactions in scanning probe microscopy
ideas.description: This research direction develops a computational framework to simulate the coupled evolution of a scanning probe microscope tip and a chiral semiconducting nanotube during a scan. The model integrates first-principles calculations of the nanotube's chirality-dependent dielectric tensor with molecular dynamics and charge redistribution models for the tip apex. This allows the investigation of how the tip's changing geometry and electronic state dynamically perturbs the nanotube's local dielectric properties and polarization, which in turn modifies the tip-sample force and tunneling current in a chirality-specific manner. The goal is to predict and explain scan artifacts, measurement hysteresis, and chirality-dependent contrast that arise from this real-time interplay, moving beyond static models of either the tip or the nanotube alone.

ideas.title: Multiscale Explicit-Continuum Model for Dielectric Screening at Nanotube Interfaces
ideas.description: This approach develops a hybrid computational framework that explicitly models the atomic structure and dynamics of the immediate nanotube environment within a dielectric continuum. The nanotube and its first solvation or adsorption layer are treated with quantum mechanical accuracy, coupled to classical polarizable molecules, while the bulk solvent or substrate is modeled as a frequency-dependent dielectric. This enables the prediction of how specific molecular interactions, orientation, and local field fluctuations at the interface quantitatively alter optical spectra, charge transfer rates, and exciton binding energies beyond a simple static dielectric constant.

ideas.title: Spatially Resolved GW-Validated Dielectric Mapping in Nanotubes via Scanning Probe Microscopy
ideas.description: This research direction combines high-accuracy GW computations of the dielectric function for specific carbon nanotube chiralities and defect structures with spatially resolved scanning capacitance microscopy (SCM). The GW-calculated dielectric response serves as a quantitative reference to deconvolute the local capacitive signals measured by SCM, transforming it from a qualitative imaging tool into a quantitative nanoscale dielectric probe. This allows for direct experimental mapping and validation of theoretically predicted dielectric oscillations near topological defects, heterojunctions, and tube ends. Consequently, the platform enables the discovery of new electronic edge states and correlation effects by comparing deviations between measured maps and GW predictions for pristine structures.

ideas.title: Deconvolving Local Dielectric Response in Nanotube Bundles Using Multi-Apex Probes
ideas.description: This research direction develops advanced scanning probe microscopy (SPM) techniques to measure the local dielectric response within carbon nanotube bundles or ropes. By employing tips with unknown or mixed apex geometries and developing signal deconvolution algorithms, the method can isolate the distinct capacitive contributions from individual nanotubes and their junctions from the complex, convoluted signal. This allows for the direct experimental investigation of how dielectric screening evolves from a single nanotube to a bundled architecture, revealing the impact of inter-tube distance, twist angle, and bundle defects on collective electrostatic behavior. The approach bridges the gap between atomically-precise tip characterization and mesoscale, heterogeneous material properties.

ideas.title: Substrate-Modified Excitonic Dielectric Screening for Complex Surfaces
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the Bethe-Salpeter equation (BSE) for excitonic effects with the study of complex substrates, aiming to compute the dielectric response of adsorbate-covered or reconstructed surfaces. The core novelty is to explicitly model how the altered electronic structure and symmetry of the substrate surface (e.g., a reconstruction on MoS2 or graphene with adatoms) modifies the electron-hole interaction kernel in the BSE. This allows for the quantitative prediction of substrate-engineered excitonic properties, such as binding energy shifts and optical selection rules, which are critical for designing optoelectronic interfaces. The outcome is a unified framework that treats the substrate not just as a dielectric background, but as an integral, designable part of the excitonic system.

ideas.title: Investigate Inter-Tube Dielectric Screening in Nanotube Ropes via Coupled Oscillator Models
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea to investigate inter-tube dielectric screening in scaled-up nanotube ropes by employing an analogy from molecular spectroscopy. In that field, the problem of understanding energy transfer and spectral shifts in molecular aggregates is solved using coupled oscillator models, which treat interacting chromophores as a system of coupled dipoles. We adapt this solution by modeling a carbon nanotube rope as an array of coupled polarizable cylinders. This theoretical scaling approach allows us to parameterize inter-tube interactions based on geometry and calculate the collective dielectric response, directly probing how screening evolves from a single tube to a bundled rope and predicting conditions for enhanced or suppressed polarization.

ideas.title: Probe local dielectric screening in chirality-defined few-tube assemblies via nano-optical mapping
ideas.description: This study shifts from scaling up to scaling down with precision, focusing on model systems of known structure. It employs templated growth or directed assembly to create well-defined few-tube bundles, including double-wall nanotubes and small ropes with specified chiralities. Using scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (s-SNOM) and related nano-spectroscopic techniques, the experiment directly maps the local dielectric function and plasmonic response with nanoscale spatial resolution. This approach directly correlates specific inter-tube geometries and electronic structures with measured screening effects, isolating the fundamental physics from ensemble averaging and disorder.

ideas.title: Adapting Helical Antenna Theory to Model Chiral Nanotube Dielectrics
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to examine the dielectric properties of chiral semiconducting nanotubes by drawing an analogy from RF engineering. The analogous problem is calculating the electromagnetic response of a helical antenna, which is solved by decomposing its current into axial and circumferential modes to predict radiation and polarization. We adapt this solution by treating the chiral nanotube's atomic lattice as a nanoscale electromagnetic helix. The adapted idea is to employ a similar decomposition of the nanotube's electronic polarization, using coupled-mode theory or modified tight-binding models, to calculate its full anisotropic, frequency-dependent dielectric tensor. This will provide a theoretical framework to predict optical activity, circular dichroism, and chiral plasmonic effects for a broad class of chiral nanotubes, moving beyond the limited zigzag case.

ideas.title: A hybrid DFT-GW-BSE framework for predictive first-principles dielectric properties
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the fundamental limitations of standard DFT for dielectric calculations by implementing a sequential, first-principles workflow. It first optimizes geometry and obtains a improved ground state using a hybrid density functional. The electronic structure is then corrected via the GW approximation to yield accurate band gaps. Finally, the optical absorption and frequency-dependent dielectric tensor are computed by solving the Bethe-Salpeter Equation, which includes electron-hole interactions critical for optical response. This integrated framework provides a robust, predictive methodology for dielectric constants and spectra from first principles.

ideas.title: Use machine-learned force fields for first-principles dielectric property calculations
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational protein folding—using machine-learned force fields trained on quantum mechanical data—to the problem of calculating dielectric properties from first principles. In biophysics, expensive ab initio calculations are used to generate training data for small systems, which then inform fast, accurate machine learning models for large-scale molecular dynamics. Similarly, we propose training a machine learning model (like a graph neural network) on a dataset of DFT-calculated dielectric properties for diverse atomic configurations. This model would learn the mapping between atomic structure and dielectric response, enabling rapid, first-principles-accurate predictions for large, complex material systems that are currently computationally prohibitive for direct DFT simulation, thereby expanding the scope of first-principles dielectric property exploration.

ideas.title: Apply Bethe-Salpeter equation for excitonic effects
ideas.description: Incorporate excitonic physics into the dielectric response calculation by method transfer from quantum chromodynamics. The analogous problem in QCD is describing color-confined hadrons (like mesons) from quark-gluon dynamics, solved using the relativistic Bethe-Salpeter equation to find bound-state wavefunctions. Adapting this solution, we propose to set up and solve the Bethe-Salpeter equation for the electron-hole two-particle Green's function in a solid, using a statically screened Coulomb interaction as the kernel. This will yield excitonic eigenstates and binding energies, which are then used to build a modified polarization function that includes these bound pairs, thereby producing a dielectric function that accurately captures excitonic effects like enhanced absorption peaks and modified optical spectra.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of STM Tips for Excitonic Image Engineering
ideas.description: This research direction combines an inverse problem approach for STM tip design with first-principles excitonic physics. The core idea is to compute the necessary tip geometry that will produce a pre-defined STM image pattern for a material where excitonic effects dominate the local dielectric response, calculated via the Bethe-Salpeter equation. This creates a closed-loop framework: starting from a desired exciton-influenced contrast map, the system inversely solves for the tip shape whose electronic structure interacts with the sample's excitonic states to generate that specific image. This synergy allows for the purposeful engineering of STM measurements to selectively enhance, suppress, or reshape the apparent signatures of excitons and other many-body phenomena at surfaces.

ideas.title: A Computationally Tractable Embedding Framework for Screened Electron-Electron Interactions
ideas.description: This redesigned approach systematically derives a computationally manageable effective model from the full Coulomb-interacting system. It first uses ab initio downfolding techniques to construct a low-energy Hamiltonian with dynamically screened and renormalized interaction parameters, capturing the essential physics of the target energy window. This effective model is then solved using a quantum embedding method, which treats a local correlated subspace exactly while embedding it in a self-consistently determined mean-field bath representing the remaining degrees of freedom. The framework is validated by ensuring its results converge to established benchmarks for model systems and correctly reproduce key experimental observables like spectral functions and spin susceptibilities for real materials.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of STM Tips to Probe and Resolve Many-Body Correlations
ideas.description: This research direction develops an inverse design algorithm that computes the precise atomic-scale geometry of an STM tip required to generate a specific contrast pattern, but with the crucial constraint that the simulated sample contrast is derived from a many-body interacting electronic model (e.g., using DFT+U, GW, or DMFT). The core synergy is that the inverse-designed tip becomes a bespoke probe, theoretically optimized to maximize sensitivity to the specific correlation signatures (like Kondo resonances, spin-polarons, or charge order) encoded in the target pattern. This enables the direct experimental test of competing many-body models by determining if a physically realizable tip can actually produce the predicted spectroscopic image, thereby linking tip fabrication directly to the resolution of fundamental electronic structure questions.

ideas.title: Multi-modal scanning probe of dynamic dielectric oscillations in charge-ordered quantum materials
ideas.description: This redesigned approach targets specific quantum materials with established charge order, such as transition metal dichalcogenides or cuprates, where theory predicts coupled charge and lattice dynamics. The experimental system integrates atomic-resolution STM to map the static unit cell reconstruction with phase-resolved, pump-probe ultrafast spectroscopy (e.g., time-resolved optical reflectivity or ultrafast electron diffraction) to directly probe the temporal evolution of the dielectric function following a tailored excitation. This combined spatial and temporal measurement isolates the oscillatory component of the charge distribution from the static background, directly testing theoretical predictions of collective electronic modes within the ordered lattice.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Misfolding Analysis to Study Defect-Induced Dielectric Changes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodological framework from structural biology, where the impact of point mutations on protein function is systematically studied, to the context of defects in 2D materials. The analogous problem in protein science involves introducing specific, known amino acid changes (defects) and using spectroscopic probes to understand how that local misfolding alters the protein's global stability and activity. The adapted research idea treats vacancies or Stone-Wales defects as analogous to point mutations in a crystalline 'protein.' The solution involves deliberately engineering materials with specific, quantified defect types and densities, then employing spatially resolved dielectric spectroscopy (e.g., scanning probe microscopy) to map how the local dielectric polarizability is altered at the defect site. This data is then used to build computational models that predict how these local perturbations summate or interact to change the macroscopic dielectric constant, directly challenging the assumption that the dielectric response is a simple average over a perfect lattice.

ideas.title: Field-Dependent Dielectric Tomography via Inverted Scanning Probe Microscopy
ideas.description: This research direction inverts the standard scanning probe microscopy paradigm by using a well-characterized tip to solve for an unknown substrate's structure. Crucially, it integrates the study of dielectric response under high electric fields directly into this inversion process. By analyzing the tip-sample interaction forces or currents across a range of applied bias voltages, the method can deconvolve both the atomic-scale topography and the local, effective dielectric constant as a function of the strong, localized field. This enables the creation of combined 3D structural and field-dependent dielectric property maps for complex or hidden interfaces, which is impossible with either approach alone.

ideas.title: Automated High-Bias Tip Spectroscopy for Voltage-Dependent Orbital Imaging
ideas.description: This research direction combines the drive to expand the operational bias voltage range in scanning probe microscopy with automated tip engineering. The core idea is to use algorithmic generation and testing of diverse tip apex structures not as a general library, but specifically to find tips that enable robust, high-bias (beyond ±1 V) spectroscopic imaging via correlation methods. This creates a closed-loop system where high-bias performance feedback directly guides the computational design of next-generation tips. The result is a targeted capability to probe voltage-activated electronic contrasts, such as higher-lying molecular orbitals or bulk conduction band states, with tailored tips that maximize signal and minimize instability at these extreme biases.

ideas.title: High-throughput mapping of emergent dielectric response from interacting defect ensembles
ideas.description: This research moves beyond the study of isolated defects to model the realistic, complex defect landscapes found in synthesized materials. Using large-scale density functional theory (DFT) and machine learning potentials, it screens thousands of supercell configurations containing stochastic mixtures and interactions of vacancies, Stone-Wales defects, and other relevant point defects. The approach quantifies how defect-defect interactions and percolation at varying concentrations lead to emergent, non-linear changes in the dielectric constant and loss, providing predictive design rules for defect-engineered dielectric materials.

ideas.title: Decoupling Dynamic Tip Evolution from Complex Surface States via Multi-Material Correlation Analysis
ideas.description: This research direction develops a generalized correlation analysis method that explicitly accounts for dynamic tip changes to study surfaces with complex reconstructions or adsorbates. By applying the dynamic tip model across a controlled set of diverse, well-characterized substrates, the system can learn to separate the evolving tip contribution from the true surface electronic or topographic states. This allows for the extraction of both a high-fidelity tip evolution function and a robust reconstruction of the intrinsic surface properties, even for materials where the 'true' surface is unknown a priori. The outcome is a powerful, substrate-agnostic deconvolution technique for scanning probe microscopy that increases data interpretability on technologically relevant but complex surfaces.

ideas.title: Spectroscopic Dielectric Correlation Microscopy (SDCM)
ideas.description: This fused idea develops a novel scanning probe microscopy technique that combines the spatial mapping capability of scanning capacitance microscopy (SCM) with advanced correlation analysis adapted for spectroscopic imaging modes. Instead of operating in a constant-current or constant-capacitance mode, the probe performs local capacitance-voltage (C-V) or capacitance-frequency (C-f) spectroscopy at each pixel. Correlation algorithms are then applied across the spectroscopic dimensions (e.g., voltage or frequency) to identify and map dynamic dielectric phenomena, such as localized dipole relaxations, trap state emissions, or ionic motion. This allows for the creation of spatially resolved maps of not just static dielectric constant, but the underlying kinetics and energy distributions of charge-active defects or polar entities within a material.

ideas.title: Multi-fidelity machine learning framework for scalable excitonic dielectric properties
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the computational bottleneck of the standard Bethe-Salpeter equation by constructing a surrogate model for excitonic effects on dielectric response. A low-cost, approximate BSE calculation on a strategically down-sampled k-point grid and excitonic basis provides a fast prior. A neural network is then trained to correct this prior's output to match high-fidelity benchmarks (full BSE or experiment) for a diverse training set of materials. The final system enables rapid, accurate prediction of exciton-enhanced absorption and dielectric functions for large-scale systems and high-throughput screening, where a full BSE would be intractable.

ideas.title: Investigating dielectric saturation and non-linear polarization via high-field Stark spectroscopy
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that the dielectric constant remains 1 under strong external electric fields by drawing an analogy from atomic spectroscopy's study of the non-linear Stark effect. In that analogous field, high electric fields reveal saturation and non-linear polarizability through perturbations in spectral lines. The adapted idea applies this methodology to a solid-state context: a strong, static (DC) electric field is applied to a dielectric material to drive it into a high-field regime, while a weak, high-frequency AC capacitive probe concurrently measures the complex dielectric function. By systematically increasing the DC field strength and observing deviations in the AC-measured polarization response, the study aims to map the onset of dielectric saturation and non-linear effects, directly testing the validity of the constant dielectric assumption in high-field conditions.

ideas.title: Quantifying field-dependent nonlinear permittivity and dielectric breakdown precursors in solids under high electric fields
ideas.description: This research systematically measures the effective dielectric constant (or, more precisely, the electric displacement field D as a function of applied field E) in solid dielectric materials under high external electric fields, up to but below breakdown. The experiment uses a precision, high-voltage bridge circuit to compare the capacitive response of a material sample against a vacuum reference capacitor, isolating the material's nonlinear polarization. By analyzing the higher-harmonic response and the deviation from linear D-E behavior, the study aims to characterize nonlinear susceptibility coefficients, identify pre-breakdown field regimes where the dielectric response becomes strongly field-dependent, and correlate these changes with microscopic models of polarization saturation and charge injection.

ideas.title: Machine learning for tip classification from correlated electron image simulations
ideas.description: This research direction integrates machine learning-based tip classification with advanced simulations that include electron-electron interactions. A generative model produces simulated scanning probe microscopy images for various tip geometries and a range of many-body correlation strengths. A neural network classifier is then trained on this dataset to directly predict tip geometry from experimental images, but its performance and internal representations also become sensitive to the underlying electronic correlations. This enables a novel, indirect spectroscopic capability: by analyzing the classifier's confidence or feature space, one can infer the dominant correlation effects in the sample, creating a feedback loop to refine the many-body simulation parameters for more physically accurate training data generation.

ideas.title: Compressive Sensing for High-Field Dielectric Response Reconstruction
ideas.description: This research fuses high-field dielectric spectroscopy with compressive sensing to reconstruct the material's full, non-linear dielectric response function from a sparse set of targeted simulations or experiments. Instead of exhaustively simulating the system's polarization response across all possible field strengths and frequencies, the method strategically samples the high-field regime where deviations from ε=1 are predicted to be most significant. The inherent sparsity of the dielectric function's variation in the high-dimensional parameter space (field, frequency, temperature) allows for accurate reconstruction from limited data. This approach makes it computationally feasible to systematically test fundamental field theories of dielectric breakdown and non-linear polarization in materials under extreme conditions.

ideas.title: Ultrafast scanning probe microscopy for spatiotemporal mapping of dielectric transients
ideas.description: This redesigned approach integrates an ultrafast optical pump with a time-resolved terahertz or microwave probe coupled to a scanning probe microscope tip. The system directly measures the local, time-dependent dielectric function following a controlled perturbation, moving beyond static or single-frequency capacitance measurements. It generates quantitative maps of dielectric relaxation times and oscillation amplitudes as a function of position, revealing dynamic heterogeneity in materials. This enables the study of phenomena like polaronic hopping, ferroelectric domain dynamics, and charge carrier trapping with both nanoscale spatial and picosecond temporal resolution.

ideas.title: Adapt Scanning Microwave Impedance Microscopy for Spatially Resolved Dielectric Spectroscopy
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from medical ultrasound elastography, where a low-frequency oscillation probes mechanical properties mapped by high-frequency imaging, this idea adapts scanning probe microscopy for dielectric mapping. The analogous solution involves applying a dynamic stimulus (vibration) and imaging the response. Adapted to materials science, a low-frequency AC bias is applied via a conductive AFM tip to locally modulate dielectric polarization. The system's response is then measured at each spatial point using high-frequency scanning microwave impedance microscopy (sMIM), which is sensitive to complex impedance (C and G). This technique spatially resolves local dielectric oscillations, loss, and tunability, enabling the study of dynamic dielectric phenomena in ferroelectrics, relaxors, or interfacial layers in semiconductors with nanoscale resolution.

ideas.title: Computer Vision-Guided Inverse Design of STM Tips for Target Image Synthesis
ideas.description: This research fuses computer vision-based image correlation with the inverse design of scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) tips to create an automated, iterative design framework. The system uses advanced image registration and feature matching to compute a quantitative similarity metric between a current experimental STM image and a pre-defined target image pattern. This metric then serves as the objective function for an inverse optimization algorithm that computes the necessary adjustments to the tip's atomic geometry. By iterating between imaging and computational redesign, the system can autonomously converge on a tip nanostructure that synthesizes the desired contrast pattern on a specific sample surface, enabling on-demand image engineering.

ideas.title: Inverse Tip Design via Acoustic Holography Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse problem solution from acoustic holography to Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM). In acoustics, a target sound field is used to compute the necessary speaker array configuration. Here, the target is a specific STM image contrast pattern. The tip geometry is parameterized and treated as the unknown source. Using a forward model of tunneling (e.g., based on the Tersoff-Hamann approximation or a more detailed electronic transport simulation), an inverse problem is formulated and solved with regularization techniques to compute the tip apex geometry that would produce the pre-defined image when scanning a given sample. This enables the rational design of tips for functional imaging or for creating specific spectroscopic maps.

ideas.title: Substrate-Adsorbate Decoupled Multi-Channel Correlation Spectroscopy for Complex Surfaces
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the fatal flaw of signal mixing on complex surfaces by developing a spectroscopic method that first disentangles the electronic and topographic signals originating from the substrate and the adsorbate/reconstruction layer. It employs simultaneous acquisition of local work function and conductance maps alongside topography, using lattice-tracking algorithms to isolate contributions from each structural component. The core correlation analysis is then applied independently to the purified signals from each channel and to their cross-correlation, enabling direct study of interfacial coupling. This yields a robust, interpretable framework for studying correlations on any heterogeneous or reconstructed surface.

ideas.title: Systematic Screening of Tip Material Compositions Using a High-Throughput Electrochemical Analogy
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from high-throughput electrocatalyst discovery, this idea adapts combinatorial screening to the problem of optimizing tip materials. In electrocatalysis, libraries of alloy thin films with composition gradients are rapidly tested to find optimal catalysts. Adapted here, a simulation framework would be developed to systematically model and correlate the performance of tip materials not just for pure elements like platinum and gold, but across a wide, continuous composition space of their alloys (e.g., Pt-Ir, Au-Pd, Pt-Rh). This high-throughput computational approach would automate the simulation of key properties—such as mechanical wear, electrical conductivity, and chemical reactivity—for hundreds of compositions, enabling the data-driven identification of superior, non-intuitive alloy tips tailored for specific applications in scanning probe microscopy or nano-electrical measurements.

ideas.title: Real-time tip stability monitoring and adaptive correction for robust nanoscale correlation analysis
ideas.description: This system integrates a secondary, fast spectroscopic probe (e.g., tip-enhanced Raman or local impedance) running concurrently with the primary scanning probe microscopy (SPM) measurement to monitor the tip's chemical and electronic state in real-time. A machine learning model analyzes this auxiliary data stream to detect signatures of tip change or contamination the moment they occur. Upon detection, the system either triggers an automated tip-cleaning protocol or tags the contemporaneous primary scan data for later corrective processing, ensuring that correlation analyses are performed only on data acquired under stable tip conditions.

ideas.title: Expand bias voltage range beyond ±1 V using a multi-stage signal conditioning approach
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to apply the correlation method at higher bias voltages to probe different electronic contrasts, but is limited by the typical ±1 V operational range of sensitive measurement electronics. Drawing an analogy from audio engineering, where a multi-stage signal path (pre-amp, pad, main amp) manages a wide dynamic range without distortion, we propose a similar adaptive circuit for the microscope. The adapted idea involves a multi-stage bias application and current measurement system: a sensitive, low-voltage stage performs the core correlation measurement, while a separate, switchable high-voltage stage is used to precondition the sample or tip electronic state. A protective attenuation or isolation network ensures the high-voltage transients do not damage the sensitive correlation electronics, thereby safely expanding the effective bias range and enabling new electronic contrast mechanisms at higher fields.

ideas.title: Property-Driven Design and Validation of Tip Materials via Dominant Mechanism Isolation
ideas.description: This research first identifies the dominant physical or chemical property (e.g., work function, surface energy, chemical potential) governing tip performance through targeted simulations and controlled experiments on baseline materials. A material library is then designed where this key property is systematically varied, either through alloying, coating, or surface treatment, while other factors are controlled. High-throughput simulation and correlated experimental validation are performed on this property-graded library. The outcome is a predictive model linking the isolated property to tip performance, enabling rational design of future tip materials beyond the tested set.
